abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated exceptional visual quality in video generation, making them promising for autonomous driving world modeling. However, existing video diffusion-based world models struggle with flexible-length, long-horizon predictions and integrating trajectory planning. This is because conventional video diffusion models rely on global joint distribution modeling of fixed-length frame sequences rather than sequentially constructing localized distributions at each timestep. In this work, we propose Epona, an autoregressive diffusion world model that enables localized spatiotemporal distribution modeling through two key innovations: 1) Decoupled spatiotemporal factorization that separates temporal dynamics modeling from fine-grained future world generation, and 2) Modular trajectory and video prediction that seamlessly integrate motion planning with visual modeling in an end-to-end framework. Our architecture enables high-resolution, long-duration generation while introducing a novel chain-of-forward training strategy to address error accumulation in autoregressive loops. Experimental results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance with 7.4\% FVD improvement and minutes longer prediction duration compared to prior works. The learned world model further serves as a real-time motion planner, outperforming strong end-to-end planners on NAVSIM benchmarks. Code will be publicly available at \href{this https URL}{this https URL}.

keyInsights: Combining the high-fidelity continuous representation of diffusion models with the temporal flexibility of autoregressive models is feasible for robust world modeling.
Explicitly decoupling trajectory planning from visual generation allows for real-time planning while maintaining high visual quality and shared understanding through a common temporal latent.
Implicit traffic world knowledge can be learned effectively from self-supervised future prediction tasks without explicit rule-based programming or auxiliary supervision.
originalProblem: Existing diffusion models for autonomous driving struggle with fixed-length, short-horizon video generation and lack explicit causal temporal structures.
GPT-style autoregressive methods handle temporal flexibility but suffer from visual quality degradation and limited long-horizon continuous trajectory planning due to image/action tokenization.
Autoregressive generation often faces error accumulation and content drift over long sequences, leading to inconsistent outputs.
results: Epona achieves a state-of-the-art Frechet Video Distance (FVD) of 82.8 on NuScenes and generates high-fidelity, consistent driving videos over two minutes (600 frames).
It demonstrates real-time trajectory planning, achieving the lowest 1-second collision rate of 0.01% on NuScenes and a state-of-the-art Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS) of 86.2 on NAVSIM.
The "chain-of-forward" training strategy is crucial for preventing visual degradation, improving long-term consistency, and enhancing FID scores in extended autoregressive generation.
solution: Epona uses a decoupled spatiotemporal factorization, employing a GPT-style transformer for temporal dynamics and twin diffusion transformers for fine-grained spatial rendering and trajectory generation.

It implements asynchronous multi-modal generation with parallel Diffusion Transformers (TrajDiT for N-step trajectory, VisDiT for next-frame prediction) conditioned on a shared temporal latent, enabling real-time planning.

A "chain-of-forward" training strategy mitigates autoregressive drift by periodically performing multiple forward passes using self-predicted frames as conditioned history, improving long-term consistency.
paper_summary.summary: Epona, developed by researchers from Horizon Robotics and several universities, introduces an autoregressive diffusion world model for autonomous driving that generates minutes-long, high-fidelity future driving scenes while enabling real-time, accurate trajectory planning. The model achieves a state-of-the-art FVD of 82.8 on NuScenes for video quality and a lowest 1-second collision rate of 0.01% among end-to-end planners, demonstrating implicit traffic rule understanding.

title: Epona: Autoregressive Diffusion World Model for Autonomous Driving

abstract: Scaling Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models on large-scale data offers a promising path to achieving a more generalized driving intelligence. However, VLA models are limited by a ``supervision deficit'': the vast model capacity is supervised by sparse, low-dimensional actions, leaving much of their representational power underutilized. To remedy this, we propose \textbf{DriveVLA-W0}, a training paradigm that employs world modeling to predict future images. This task generates a dense, self-supervised signal that compels the model to learn the underlying dynamics of the driving environment. We showcase the paradigm's versatility by instantiating it for two dominant VLA archetypes: an autoregressive world model for VLAs that use discrete visual tokens, and a diffusion world model for those operating on continuous visual features. Building on the rich representations learned from world modeling, we introduce a lightweight action expert to address the inference latency for real-time deployment. Extensive experiments on the NAVSIM v1/v2 benchmark and a 680x larger in-house dataset demonstrate that DriveVLA-W0 significantly outperforms BEV and VLA baselines. Crucially, it amplifies the data scaling law, showing that performance gains accelerate as the training dataset size increases.

keyInsights: World modeling provides a dense self-supervised signal that amplifies data scaling laws, enabling sustained performance improvements for VLAs as dataset size increases, where action-only models plateau.
Integrating world modeling facilitates superior generalization across different driving scenarios by compelling the model to learn transferable visual representations of the environment.

The optimal action decoding strategy is dependent on data scale, with autoregressive decoders outperforming continuous decoders on massive datasets due to their strong modeling capacity and sample efficiency.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving suffer from a "supervision deficit," as they are trained with sparse, low-dimensional action signals from high-dimensional sensory inputs.
This deficit prevents VLAs from effectively leveraging their large capacity and realizing their full potential for data scaling to achieve more generalized driving intelligence.
The large size of VLA backbones typically results in high inference latency, hindering their practical real-time deployment in autonomous vehicles.
results: DriveVLA-W0 achieved state-of-the-art performance on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks using only a single front-view camera, surpassing methods that utilize richer sensor suites.

On a 70M-frame in-house dataset, world modeling significantly amplified data scaling, improving the VQ model's Average Displacement Error by 28.8% and the ViT model's collision rate by 15.9% compared to baselines.
The lightweight Action Expert reduced inference latency by up to 63.1% compared to the full VLA backbone, enabling real-time deployment without sacrificing performance.
solution: Integrate a world modeling objective into VLA training, forcing the model to predict future visual scenes to provide dense, self-supervised signals that overcome the supervision deficit.
Introduce a lightweight Action Expert within a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to enable real-time inference by decoupling action generation from the large VLA backbone.
Instantiate the world modeling paradigm for two VLA architectural families (discrete visual tokens and continuous visual features) to demonstrate its broad applicability.
paper_summary.summary: DriveVLA-W0 introduces a world modeling paradigm for Vision-Language-Action models in autonomous driving, addressing their supervision deficit and demonstrating amplified data scaling laws with increasing dataset size. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on NAVSIM benchmarks and improves performance on a 70M-frame dataset, while also enabling real-time deployment through a lightweight action expert.

title: DriveVLA-W0: World Models Amplify Data Scaling Law in Autonomous Driving

abstract: World models can foresee the outcomes of different actions, which is of paramount importance for autonomous driving. Nevertheless, existing driving world models still have limitations in generalization to unseen environments, prediction fidelity of critical details, and action controllability for flexible application. In this paper, we present Vista, a generalizable driving world model with high fidelity and versatile controllability. Based on a systematic diagnosis of existing methods, we introduce several key ingredients to address these limitations. To accurately predict real-world dynamics at high resolution, we propose two novel losses to promote the learning of moving instances and structural information. We also devise an effective latent replacement approach to inject historical frames as priors for coherent long-horizon rollouts. For action controllability, we incorporate a versatile set of controls from high-level intentions (command, goal point) to low-level maneuvers (trajectory, angle, and speed) through an efficient learning strategy. After large-scale training, the capabilities of Vista can seamlessly generalize to different scenarios. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets show that Vista outperforms the most advanced general-purpose video generator in over 70% of comparisons and surpasses the best-performing driving world model by 55% in FID and 27% in FVD. Moreover, for the first time, we utilize the capacity of Vista itself to establish a generalizable reward for real-world action evaluation without accessing the ground truth actions.

keyInsights: Imposing explicit dynamic priors through multiple historical frames and applying specialized dynamics and structure preservation losses significantly enhances motion realism and structural detail in long-horizon predictions.

Diffusion models can be effectively adapted to exhibit versatile, multi-modal action controllability for driving scenarios through efficient, two-stage training strategies combined with parameter-efficient LoRA adapters.

A driving world model's internal prediction uncertainty can serve as an effective, self-contained, and generalizable reward signal for evaluating real-world actions without requiring explicit ground truth labels.
originalProblem: Existing driving world models struggle with generalization to unseen environments due to limitations in training data scale and geographical coverage.
Current models often lack high spatiotemporal fidelity, producing low-resolution or low-frame-rate predictions that miss critical dynamic and structural details.
Most models offer restricted action controllability, typically supporting only a single action modality, which is insufficient for the diverse requirements of autonomous driving planning.
results: Vista achieved state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes, with an FID of 6.9 and FVD of 89.4, representing a 55% and 27% improvement respectively over previous best-performing driving world models.
The model consistently generates coherent, high-fidelity future predictions up to 15 seconds at 10 Hz and 576x1024 resolution, demonstrating strong cross-dataset generalization capabilities.

Vista demonstrated effective multi-modal action controllability, quantitatively reducing trajectory differences for specific action types, and its uncertainty-based reward function successfully evaluated actions on unseen data, showing an inverse correlation with trajectory errors.
solution: A two-phase training pipeline extends the Stable Video Diffusion framework, first learning high-fidelity future prediction via dynamic prior injection (multiple historical frames) and novel dynamics and structure preservation losses.

Versatile action controllability is introduced by encoding multiple action types (low-level steering/speed, high-level commands/goal points) and injecting them into the model using LoRA adapters and an action independence training strategy.

A novel generalizable reward function is proposed, evaluating actions by estimating prediction uncertainty (conditional variance) through ensemble denoising, thereby not relying on ground truth labels or external detectors.
paper_summary.summary: Vista introduces a driving world model that generates high-fidelity, long-horizon future predictions at 10 Hz and 576x1024 resolution, capable of cross-dataset generalization and versatile control via various action modalities. The model also functions as a generalizable reward function by leveraging its prediction uncertainty to evaluate actions.

title: Vista: A Generalizable Driving World Model with High Fidelity and Versatile Controllability

abstract: Autonomous driving demands safe motion planning, especially in critical "long-tail" scenarios. Recent end-to-end autonomous driving systems leverage large language models (LLMs) as planners to improve generalizability to rare events. However, using LLMs at test time introduces high computational costs. To address this, we propose DiMA, an end-to-end autonomous driving system that maintains the efficiency of an LLM-free (or vision-based) planner while leveraging the world knowledge of an LLM. DiMA distills the information from a multi-modal LLM to a vision-based end-to-end planner through a set of specially designed surrogate tasks. Under a joint training strategy, a scene encoder common to both networks produces structured representations that are semantically grounded as well as aligned to the final planning objective. Notably, the LLM is optional at inference, enabling robust planning without compromising on efficiency. Training with DiMA results in a 37% reduction in the L2 trajectory error and an 80% reduction in the collision rate of the vision-based planner, as well as a 44% trajectory error reduction in longtail scenarios. DiMA also achieves state-of-the-art performance on the nuScenes planning benchmark.

keyInsights: Structured scene representations better than dense unstructured features for autonomous driving
Joint training of vision system and LLM helps learn semantically grounded features
Surrogate tasks like masked reconstruction and scene editing improve representation learning
Knowledge distillation aligns objectives of vision and language models
originalProblem: Vision-based autonomous driving systems struggle with rare/long-tail scenarios despite being computationally efficient
LLM-based systems are more robust but have high computational overhead at inference time
Bridging visual and language domains is complex with limited training data
results: 37% reduction in trajectory error compared to vision-only baseline
80% reduction in collision rate
44% improvement in long-tail scenario performance
State-of-the-art results on nuScenes planning benchmark
Maintains computational efficiency by not requiring LLM at inference
solution: Propose DiMA framework that jointly trains a vision-based planner and multi-modal LLM
Uses structured scene representations (BEAM tokens) as input to LLM
Introduces surrogate tasks to ground visual features in language
Distills knowledge from LLM to vision planner while keeping LLM optional at inference
paper_summary.summary: This paper introduces a framework for distilling knowledge from multi-modal language models into vision-based autonomous driving systems to improve robustness while maintaining efficiency

title: Distilling Multi-modal Large Language Models for Autonomous Driving

abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving increasingly leverages self-supervised video pretraining to learn transferable planning representations. However, pretraining video world models for scene understanding has so far brought only limited improvements. This limitation is compounded by the inherent ambiguity of driving: each scene typically provides only a single human trajectory, making it difficult to learn multimodal behaviors. In this work, we propose Drive-JEPA, a framework that integrates Video Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA) with multimodal trajectory distillation for end-to-end driving. First, we adapt V-JEPA for end-to-end driving, pretraining a ViT encoder on large-scale driving videos to produce predictive representations aligned with trajectory planning. Second, we introduce a proposal-centric planner that distills diverse simulator-generated trajectories alongside human trajectories, with a momentum-aware selection mechanism to promote stable and safe behavior. When evaluated on NAVSIM, the V-JEPA representation combined with a simple transformer-based decoder outperforms prior methods by 3 PDMS in the perception-free setting. The complete Drive-JEPA framework achieves 93.3 PDMS on v1 and 87.8 EPDMS on v2, setting a new state-of-the-art.

keyInsights: V-JEPA-based self-supervised pretraining on driving videos can learn highly effective, planning-aligned visual representations, as evidenced by strong "perception-free" driving performance.

Distilling multimodal trajectories from rule-based simulators into the learning process successfully addresses the supervision bottleneck of single human demonstrations, fostering the ability to learn diverse driving behaviors.
A momentum-aware trajectory selection mechanism is essential to balance the increased diversity from multimodal supervision with the need for temporal consistency and driving comfort.
originalProblem: Self-supervised video pretraining for autonomous driving planning has shown limited improvements, with methods being either computationally intensive or prone to representation collapse.

Imitation learning for autonomous driving faces a "supervision bottleneck" where datasets provide only a single human trajectory per scene, hindering the learning of diverse and robust driving policies.
results: Drive-JEPA achieved state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v2 with 87.8 EPDMS and on Bench2Drive with a 64.52 Driving Score in closed-loop simulation.

V-JEPA-based pretraining learned robust planning-aligned representations, enabling 89.0 PDMS in a "perception-free" setting, significantly surpassing other perception-free and some perception-reliant methods.

Multimodal Trajectories Distillation (MTD) increased trajectory diversity from 24% to 40%, and the momentum-aware selection module boosted Extended Comfort from 47.9 to 84.8, leading to a higher overall EPDMS.
solution: Employs a Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder, pretrained with the V-JEPA objective on a large-scale curated driving video dataset, to learn robust planning-aligned visual representations.

Introduces Multimodal Trajectories Distillation (MTD) to generate diverse "pseudo-teacher" trajectories by scoring a trajectory vocabulary using a rule-based simulator, overcoming the single human trajectory limitation.
Integrates a momentum-aware trajectory selection module that uses a neural scorer and a comfort term to select temporally consistent and comfortable driving plans from diverse proposals.
paper_summary.summary: Drive-JEPA integrates V-JEPA video pretraining with multimodal trajectory distillation to develop an end-to-end autonomous driving framework, achieving state-of-the-art performance on NAVSIM v2 with 87.8 EPDMS and Bench2Drive with a 64.52 Driving Score using only a single front-view camera. This approach effectively learns planning-aligned representations and diverse driving behaviors.

title: Drive-JEPA: Video JEPA Meets Multimodal Trajectory Distillation for End-to-End Driving

abstract: Autonomous driving (AD) systems struggle in long-tail scenarios due to limited world knowledge and weak visual dynamic modeling. Existing vision-language-action (VLA)-based methods cannot leverage unlabeled videos for visual causal learning, while world model-based methods lack reasoning capabilities from large language models. In this paper, we construct multiple specialized datasets providing reasoning and planning annotations for complex scenarios. Then, a unified Understanding-Generation-Planning framework, named UniUGP, is proposed to synergize scene reasoning, future video generation, and trajectory planning through a hybrid expert architecture. By integrating pre-trained VLMs and video generation models, UniUGP leverages visual dynamics and semantic reasoning to enhance planning performance. Taking multi-frame observations and language instructions as input, it produces interpretable chain-of-thought reasoning, physically consistent trajectories, and coherent future videos. We introduce a four-stage training strategy that progressively builds these capabilities across multiple existing AD datasets, along with the proposed specialized datasets. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in perception, reasoning, and decision-making, with superior generalization to challenging long-tail situations.

keyInsights: Unifying Vision-Language-Action (VLA) and world models within a hybrid expert architecture allows AD systems to leverage both high-level reasoning and visual causal dynamics, improving performance in complex long-tail scenarios.
The generation expert, by modeling future visual dynamics, compels the VLA model to learn visual causal inference, enhancing the prediction of potential dangers and overall driving safety.

A multi-stage training strategy, combined with specialized datasets and pre-trained models, effectively aligns and integrates diverse capabilities, leading to more robust and interpretable autonomous driving decisions.
originalProblem: Autonomous driving systems frequently fail in rare, unusual, or safety-critical 'long-tail' scenarios due to limited world knowledge and insufficient visual dynamic modeling.
Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, despite strong reasoning, cannot fully exploit vast unlabeled driving videos to learn robust visual causal inference.
World models excel at predicting future visual dynamics for planning but lack the sophisticated reasoning, world knowledge, and interactive potential offered by large language models.
results: UniUGP significantly outperformed baselines on a new long-tail benchmark, achieving 89.3% for small object recognition, 88.6% for accident subject relationship, and 95.8% for accident prediction.
It achieved competitive motion planning performance on nuScenes with an average L2 distance of 1.23m and a collision rate of 0.33% using only a front camera, improving over methods like Doe-1.

The model achieved an FID of 7.4 and FVD of 75.9 for future frame generation on NuScenes, outperforming state-of-the-art models, and scored 0.59 on the DriveLM GVQA Benchmark, surpassing FSDrive and OmniDrive.
solution: UniUGP proposes a unified framework with a hybrid expert architecture that integrates understanding (via Qwen2.5-VL), planning (via flow matching), and generation (via DiT blocks from Wan2.1).
Constructs multiple specialized datasets from challenging long-tail driving videos, annotated for perception, causal Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, planning, and instruction following.

Employs a four-stage progressive training strategy to sequentially build foundational scene understanding, visual dynamic modeling, text-based reasoning, and multi-capability fusion using diverse autonomous driving datasets.
paper_summary.summary: UniUGP presents a unified framework for end-to-end autonomous driving, integrating scene understanding, future video generation, and trajectory planning through a hybrid expert architecture. This approach enhances interpretability with Chain-of-Thought reasoning and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in challenging long-tail scenarios and multimodal capabilities across various benchmarks.

title: UniUGP: Unifying Understanding, Generation, and Planing For End-to-end Autonomous Driving

abstract: World models have become crucial for autonomous driving, as they learn how scenarios evolve over time to address the long-tail challenges of the real world. However, current approaches relegate world models to limited roles: they operate within ostensibly unified architectures that still keep world prediction and motion planning as decoupled processes. To bridge this gap, we propose DriveLaW, a novel paradigm that unifies video generation and motion planning. By directly injecting the latent representation from its video generator into the planner, DriveLaW ensures inherent consistency between high-fidelity future generation and reliable trajectory planning. Specifically, DriveLaW consists of two core components: DriveLaW-Video, our powerful world model that generates high-fidelity forecasting with expressive latent representations, and DriveLaW-Act, a diffusion planner that generates consistent and reliable trajectories from the latent of DriveLaW-Video, with both components optimized by a three-stage progressive training strategy. The power of our unified paradigm is demonstrated by new state-of-the-art results across both tasks. DriveLaW not only advances video prediction significantly, surpassing best-performing work by 33.3% in FID and 1.8% in FVD, but also achieves a new record on the NAVSIM planning benchmark.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Leveraging expressive latent representations from a video generator provides superior 'driving cognition priors' for trajectory planning, significantly enhancing decision-making robustness.

A novel noise reinjection mechanism addresses perceptual degradation in high-speed driving videos by selectively re-injecting noise into high-frequency regions, restoring sharpness and structural consistency.

Converting ego-vehicle kinematics into natural language prompts for motion conditioning effectively integrates control signals into the video transformer, enhancing generalization and interpretability.
originalProblem: Existing autonomous driving systems exhibit brittleness in complex, long-tail scenarios, posing challenges for safe deployment.
Current world model approaches often decouple video prediction from motion planning, or only indirectly supervise planning, leading to a 'disconnect' between visual understanding and action.
Previously 'unified' architectures still treat video generation and policy heads as separate modules, failing to fully leverage rich internal latent representations for planning.
results: DriveLaW achieved state-of-the-art video generation quality on nuScenes, scoring an FID of 4.6 (33.3% improvement over Epona) and an FVD of 81.3 (1.8% improvement).
The system set a new state-of-the-art record for closed-loop motion planning on the NAVSIM benchmark with a PDMS of 89.1, outperforming prior methods without post-training or external scorers.

Ablation studies confirmed that video generator latent features improved planning performance by 5.0 PDMS over BEV features and 2.6 PDMS over VLM hidden states, demonstrating their superior effectiveness as planning conditions.
solution: DriveLaW employs a novel 'chained' architecture, serially connecting a Spatiotemporal World Generator (DriveLaW-Video) with a Diffusion-Based Planner (DriveLaW-Act).
It directly injects the denoised video latents from DriveLaW-Video into DriveLaW-Act, ensuring the action model explicitly leverages the world understanding from video generation.
A three-stage progressive training strategy manages the optimization tension between high-fidelity video synthesis and stable planning, systematically building model capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: DriveLaW introduces an autonomous driving system that integrates video generation and motion planning by serially feeding latent representations from its world model directly into a diffusion planner. This framework enables the generation of high-fidelity future driving videos and achieves new state-of-the-art performance in closed-loop motion planning on the NAVSIM benchmark.

title: DriveLaW:Unifying Planning and Video Generation in a Latent Driving World

abstract:
Achieving human-like driving behaviors in complex open-world environments is
a critical challenge in autonomous driving. Contemporary learning-based
planning approaches such as imitation learning methods often struggle to
balance competing objectives and lack of safety assurance,due to limited
adaptability and inadequacy in learning complex multi-modal behaviors commonly
exhibited in human planning, not to mention their strong reliance on the
fallback strategy with predefined rules. We propose a novel transformer-based
Diffusion Planner for closed-loop planning, which can effectively model
multi-modal driving behavior and ensure trajectory quality without any
rule-based refinement. Our model supports joint modeling of both prediction and
planning tasks under the same architecture, enabling cooperative behaviors
between vehicles. Moreover, by learning the gradient of the trajectory score
function and employing a flexible classifier guidance mechanism, Diffusion
Planner effectively achieves safe and adaptable planning behaviors. Evaluations
on the large-scale real-world autonomous planning benchmark nuPlan and our
newly collected 200-hour delivery-vehicle driving dataset demonstrate that
Diffusion Planner achieves state-of-the-art closed-loop performance with robust
transferability in diverse driving styles.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Diffusion models inherently capture complex, multi-modal data distributions, enabling diverse and human-like driving behaviors.
Training-free classifier guidance allows for flexible, runtime adjustment of driving styles (e.g., safety, comfort, speed) without additional training.
A unified architecture for joint prediction and planning promotes cooperative behaviors between the ego vehicle and surrounding agents, simplifying system design.
originalProblem: Traditional imitation learning struggles to capture the multi-modal nature of human driving behaviors, often producing averaged or single-modal outputs.
Learning-based planners frequently require rule-based fallback strategies or post-processing to ensure trajectory quality and safety in out-of-distribution scenarios.
Balancing competing objectives like safety, efficiency, and comfort in learning-based methods often leads to conflicts and difficulties in post-training adaptation of driving styles.
results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among learning-based baselines on the nuPlan benchmark and demonstrates strong transferability on a newly collected 200-hour delivery-vehicle dataset.

Generates high-quality, smooth ego planning trajectories and accurate predictions for neighboring vehicles, effectively modeling multi-modal driving behaviors such as different turns at an intersection.

Validates the effectiveness of training-free classifier guidance, allowing flexible control over driving attributes like target speed and comfort, and enabling the combination of multiple guidance objectives during inference.
solution: A transformer-based diffusion model (Diffusion Planner) is designed for joint future trajectory generation of both the ego vehicle and its neighboring vehicles.
Environmental information, including historical states, lane data, and navigation details, is integrated into the diffusion architecture using MLP-Mixers and transformer encoders.

A training-free classifier guidance mechanism, based on differentiable energy functions (e.g., collision avoidance, target speed, comfort, drivable area), is employed during inference to enforce versatile and controllable driving behaviors.
paper_summary.summary: A novel Diffusion Planner leverages transformer-based diffusion models for high-performance motion planning in autonomous driving, achieving state-of-the-art results on the nuPlan benchmark by enabling multi-modal behavior modeling and flexible guidance without rule-based refinement.

title: Diffusion-Based Planning for Autonomous Driving with Flexible Guidance

abstract: Autonomous driving world models are expected to work effectively across three core dimensions: state, action, and reward. Existing models, however, are typically restricted to limited state modalities, short video sequences, imprecise action control, and a lack of reward awareness. In this paper, we introduce OmniNWM, an omniscient panoramic navigation world model that addresses all three dimensions within a unified framework. For state, OmniNWM jointly generates panoramic videos of RGB, semantics, metric depth, and 3D occupancy. A flexible forcing strategy enables high-quality long-horizon auto-regressive generation. For action, we introduce a normalized panoramic Plucker ray-map representation that encodes input trajectories into pixel-level signals, enabling highly precise and generalizable control over panoramic video generation. Regarding reward, we move beyond learning reward functions with external image-based models: instead, we leverage the generated 3D occupancy to directly define rule-based dense rewards for driving compliance and safety. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OmniNWM achieves state-of-the-art performance in video generation, control accuracy, and long-horizon stability, while providing a reliable closed-loop evaluation framework through occupancy-grounded rewards. Project page is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Jointly generating diverse multi-modal outputs (RGB, semantic, depth, 3D occupancy) provides a more comprehensive and explicit understanding of complex 3D driving environments than single-modality approaches.

Normalizing Plücker ray-map representations offers a unified, pixel-level action encoding that enables highly precise camera control and strong zero-shot generalization across various camera configurations and datasets.

Leveraging generated 3D occupancy to derive intrinsic, rule-based dense rewards provides a precise, interpretable, and fine-grained feedback mechanism, crucial for effectively training and evaluating autonomous driving policies in a closed-loop simulation.
originalProblem: Existing autonomous driving world models exhibit limited state modalities, primarily relying on single-modality RGB videos, which lack rich geometric and semantic 3D information.

Current generative capabilities are often restricted to short temporal horizons, and action encoding uses sparse or computationally heavy representations, hindering long-term planning and precise viewpoint manipulation.

Reward functions, if integrated, typically depend on external, image-based models, leading to imprecise feedback and a lack of fine-grained environmental understanding for evaluating driving safety and compliance.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art RGB video generation (FID 5.45, FVD 23.63) and panoramic depth generation (Abs. Rel. 0.23, δ < 1.25 of 0.81) on NuScenes, significantly outperforming prior methods.
Set a new state-of-the-art for semantic occupancy prediction on NuScenes-Occupancy (IoU 33.3, mIoU 19.8), surpassing both prior generative and many discriminative camera/LiDAR-based approaches.

Demonstrated effective closed-loop trajectory planning, with OmniNWM-VLA achieving competitive Scenario Pass Rates and providing precise, fine-grained, occupancy-grounded rewards for policy evaluation.
Exhibited strong zero-shot generalization capabilities, generating high-quality panoramic videos and maintaining control across new datasets and varying camera configurations without fine-tuning.
solution: A unified framework centered on a Panoramic Diffusion Transformer (PDiT) and a pre-trained 3D VAE generates pixel-aligned panoramic RGB, semantic segmentation, metric depth, and 3D occupancy.
A novel normalized panoramic Plücker ray-map representation provides precise and generalizable pixel-level camera control by encoding trajectories with scale- and pose-invariant properties.
Intrinsic, rule-based dense rewards are directly computed from the generated 3D semantic occupancy, evaluating driving safety, efficiency, and compliance for fine-grained policy evaluation.
A flexible forcing strategy with multi-level noise injection is employed to mitigate error accumulation and ensure robust, long-term auto-regressive generation stability.
paper_summary.summary: OmniNWM introduces a unified driving navigation world model that jointly generates multi-modal panoramic videos, including RGB, semantic, and depth, alongside 3D occupancy. The model enables precise camera control through a normalized Plücker ray-map and integrates occupancy-grounded intrinsic rewards, achieving state-of-the-art results in generation quality and closed-loop planning evaluation.

title: OmniNWM: Omniscient Driving Navigation World Models

abstract: End-to-end (E2E) autonomous driving has recently attracted increasing interest in unifying Vision-Language-Action (VLA) with World Models to enhance decision-making and forward-looking imagination. However, existing methods fail to effectively unify future scene evolution and action planning within a single architecture due to inadequate sharing of latent states, limiting the impact of visual imagination on action decisions. To address this limitation, we propose DriveWorld-VLA, a novel framework that unifies world modeling and planning within a latent space by tightly integrating VLA and world models at the representation level, which enables the VLA planner to benefit directly from holistic scene-evolution modeling and reducing reliance on dense annotated supervision. Additionally, DriveWorld-VLA incorporates the latent states of the world model as core decision-making states for the VLA planner, facilitating the planner to assess how candidate actions impact future scene evolution. By conducting world modeling entirely in the latent space, DriveWorld-VLA supports controllable, action-conditioned imagination at the feature level, avoiding expensive pixel-level rollouts. Extensive open-loop and closed-loop evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of DriveWorld-VLA, which achieves state-of-the-art performance with 91.3 PDMS on NAVSIMv1, 86.8 EPDMS on NAVSIMv2, and 0.16 3-second average collision rate on nuScenes. Code and models will be released in this https URL.

keyInsights: Tightly integrating VLA models with world models in a shared latent space fosters a deeper internalization of physical laws and environmental dynamics, directly benefiting decision-making.
Enabling action-conditioned causal reasoning through a Diffusion Transformer allows for proactive "what-if" scenario evaluation, moving beyond reactive planning.
A progressive, multi-stage training paradigm is crucial for stabilizing the joint optimization of generative world models and action planning, incrementally unlocking full system capabilities.
originalProblem: Current autonomous driving systems, including E2E and VLA-WM integrations, struggle to effectively unify future scene evolution prediction with action planning, leading to limited causal reasoning.
Existing world model integrations often treat the world model as an external simulator or lack explicit action-conditioned "what-if" imagination, hindering proactive, long-horizon decision-making.
Autonomous driving development often relies on computationally expensive pixel-level future rollouts and extensive, dense supervised data annotation.
results: DriveWorld-VLA achieved state-of-the-art performance on closed-loop NAVSIMv1 (91.3 PDMS) and NAVSIMv2 (86.8 EPDMS) benchmarks, showing high safety compliance (e.g., 99.1% No Collision on NAVSIMv1) and effective ego progress.

The system demonstrated competitive performance on the nuScenes open-loop planning benchmark, with an average L2 distance of 0.61m and a remarkably low average collision rate of 0.16%, surpassing several specialized baselines in collision avoidance.

Ablation studies validated that each stage of the progressive training paradigm contributes significantly to performance, and that VLM optimization and feature-level supervision are crucial for the model's multi-modal fusion and representation capabilities.
solution: A unified latent-space world modeling framework tightly integrates VLA models with World Models, leveraging a Large Language Model's hidden states as a shared latent space for both imagination and action prediction.

A Diffusion Transformer (DiT) enables action-conditioned "what-if" reasoning, allowing the system to simulate and evaluate the long-term consequences of multiple candidate actions in the shared latent space.

A three-stage progressive training paradigm (VLA & WM joint training, action controllability fine-tuning, and future-guided evaluation & refinement with reward feedback) incrementally builds capabilities and stabilizes joint optimization.
paper_summary.summary: DriveWorld-VLA introduces a unified latent-space world modeling framework that integrates Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, enabling autonomous driving systems to perform action-conditioned "what-if" reasoning for proactive planning. The system demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on NAVSIMv1, NAVSIMv2, and nuScenes benchmarks, achieving high safety compliance and low collision rates.

title: DriveWorld-VLA: Unified Latent-Space World Modeling with Vision-Language-Action for Autonomous Driving

abstract: Autonomous driving requires reasoning about how the environment evolves and planning actions accordingly. Existing world-model-based approaches typically predict future scenes first and plan afterwards, resulting in open-loop imagination that may drift from the actual decision process. In this paper, we present Uni-World VLA, a unified vision-language-action (VLA) model that tightly interleaves future frame prediction and trajectory planning. Instead of generating a full world rollout before planning, our model alternates between predicting future frames and ego actions step by step, allowing planning decisions to be continuously conditioned on the imagined future observations. This interleaved generation forms a closed-loop interaction between world modeling and control, enabling more adaptive decision-making in dynamic traffic scenarios. In addition, we incorporate monocular depth information into frames to provide stronger geometric cues for world modeling, improving long-horizon scene prediction. Experiments on the NAVSIM benchmark show that our approach achieves competitive closed-loop planning performance while producing high-fidelity future frame predictions. These results demonstrate that tightly coupling world prediction and planning is a promising direction for scalable VLA driving systems.

keyInsights: A continuous, interleaved feedback loop between world state prediction and ego-vehicle action planning is essential for adaptive decision-making in dynamic driving environments, overcoming limitations of static future rollouts.
Integrating monocular depth information significantly improves the geometric understanding and temporal consistency of predicted future scenes, crucial for robust long-horizon planning.

Optimizing the frequency and historical context of visual input (contextual and dynamic tokens) is critical for balancing detailed scene semantics with fine-grained motion cues, leading to better planning and generation performance.
originalProblem: Autonomous driving systems using world models often employ 'open-loop imagination,' where future scene predictions are static and do not dynamically adapt to the ego vehicle's evolving actions.
This 'frozen hallucination' leads to outdated visual evidence for planning in dynamic, interactive scenarios, hindering adaptive decision-making.
Prior world models primarily rely on RGB inputs, which can lack sufficient geometric cues for high-fidelity, long-horizon scene and motion prediction.
results: Uni-World VLA achieved the highest Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS) of 89.4 on the NAVSIM benchmark, outperforming state-of-the-art methods, including multi-sensor approaches.
The model demonstrated competitive video generation quality, achieving a Fréchet Video Distance (FVD) of 141.8 for 4-second future rollouts.

Ablation studies confirmed that interleaved future-frame generation and monocular depth integration significantly improve both planning performance (e.g., increased Ego Progress and Time-To-Collision) and visual fidelity of predictions.
solution: Uni-World VLA employs a unified autoregressive VLA model that tightly interleaves future frame prediction and trajectory planning in a step-wise, closed-loop fashion.
It integrates monocular depth maps, extracted by Depth Anything 3, into historical visual inputs through specialized depth encoders and cross-attention fusion.
The model is trained with joint supervision on future visual token generation (using Dynamic Focal Loss) and trajectory prediction (L1 loss), ensuring temporal consistency.
paper_summary.summary: Uni-World VLA introduces a unified Vision-Language-Action model for autonomous driving that tightly interleaves future world state prediction and ego-vehicle trajectory planning within a closed-loop feedback mechanism. This approach, incorporating monocular depth, achieved a top Predictive Driver Model Score of 89.4 and a Fréchet Video Distance of 141.8 on the NAVSIM benchmark, demonstrating improved adaptive decision-making and visual fidelity.

title: Uni-World VLA: Interleaved World Modeling and Planning for Autonomous Driving

abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving directly generates planning trajectories from raw sensor data, yet it typically relies on costly perception supervision to extract scene information. A critical research challenge arises: constructing an informative driving world model to enable perception annotation-free, end-to-end planning via self-supervised learning. In this paper, we present World4Drive, an end-to-end autonomous driving framework that employs vision foundation models to build latent world models for generating and evaluating multi-modal planning trajectories. Specifically, World4Drive first extracts scene features, including driving intention and world latent representations enriched with spatial-semantic priors provided by vision foundation models. It then generates multi-modal planning trajectories based on current scene features and driving intentions and predicts multiple intention-driven future states within the latent space. Finally, it introduces a world model selector module to evaluate and select the best trajectory. We achieve perception annotation-free, end-to-end planning through self-supervised alignment between actual future observations and predicted observations reconstructed from the latent space. World4Drive achieves state-of-the-art performance without manual perception annotations on both the open-loop nuScenes and closed-loop NavSim benchmarks, demonstrating an 18.1\% relative reduction in L2 error, 46.7% lower collision rate, and 3.75 faster training convergence. Codes will be accessed at this https URL.

author_info.institution: Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences

keyInsights: Self-supervised learning for autonomous driving can be significantly enhanced by integrating pre-trained vision foundation models to provide robust spatial and semantic understanding without explicit manual annotations.
Explicitly modeling multi-modal driving intentions within a predictive world model allows for more robust and human-like planning by evaluating diverse future possibilities.
Combining intention-awareness and rich physical priors within a latent world model drastically improves training efficiency and overall planning performance for end-to-end autonomous driving systems.
originalProblem: End-to-end autonomous driving systems typically rely on costly and time-consuming manual perception annotations (e.g., 3D bounding boxes, HD maps), which hinders scalability and deployment.
Prior self-supervised latent world models for autonomous driving, like LAW, suffer from slow training convergence and suboptimal performance due to limited spatial-semantic understanding.
Existing latent world models struggle to adequately capture the uncertainty and multi-modal nature of human-like driving intentions, leading to less robust planning decisions.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance among perception annotation-free methods on nuScenes, with an 18.0% relative reduction in average L2 error and a 46.7% relative reduction in average collision rate compared to the LAW baseline.
Demonstrated 3.75x faster training convergence speed and a 1.18x improvement in peak performance compared to LAW on the nuScenes dataset.

Exhibited enhanced robustness across diverse driving conditions (e.g., night, rain) with dramatic reductions in collision rates, attributed to improved high-dimensional spatial and semantic understanding.
solution: Developed an intention-aware physical latent world model that directly processes multi-view images to predict planning trajectories without explicit perception annotations.
Integrated pre-trained vision foundation models (Grounded-SAM for pseudo-semantic labels, Metric3D v2 for 3D depth) to embed rich spatial-semantic priors into the latent world representation.
Implemented an intention encoder and a world model selector to enable multi-modal planning, allowing the system to generate and evaluate multiple plausible trajectories based on different intentions.
paper_summary.summary: World4Drive presents an end-to-end autonomous driving framework that generates planning trajectories directly from raw sensor data, eliminating the need for manual perception annotations by leveraging intention-aware physical latent world models. This system achieves a 46.7% relative reduction in collision rate and 3.75x faster training convergence compared to previous self-supervised methods on the nuScenes dataset.

title: World4Drive: End-to-End Autonomous Driving via Intention-aware Physical Latent World Model

abstract: Advanced end-to-end autonomous driving systems predict other vehicles' motions and plan ego vehicle's trajectory. The world model that can foresee the outcome of the trajectory has been used to evaluate the autonomous driving system. However, existing world models predominantly emphasize the trajectory of the ego vehicle and leave other vehicles uncontrollable. This limitation hinders their ability to realistically simulate the interaction between the ego vehicle and the driving scenario. In this paper, we propose a driving World Model named EOT-WM, unifying Ego-Other vehicle Trajectories in videos for driving simulation. Specifically, it remains a challenge to match multiple trajectories in the BEV space with each vehicle in the video to control the video generation. We first project ego-other vehicle trajectories in the BEV space into the image coordinate for vehicle-trajectory match via pixel positions. Then, trajectory videos are encoded by the Spatial-Temporal Variational Auto Encoder to align with driving video latents spatially and temporally in the unified visual space. A trajectory-injected diffusion Transformer is further designed to denoise the noisy video latents for video generation with the guidance of ego-other vehicle trajectories. In addition, we propose a metric based on control latent similarity to evaluate the controllability of trajectories. Extensive experiments are conducted on the nuScenes dataset, and the proposed model outperforms the state-of-the-art method by 30% in FID and 55% in FVD. The model can also predict unseen driving scenes with self-produced trajectories.

keyInsights: Representing trajectories as videos and aligning them with scene videos in a shared latent space effectively bridges the modality gap for precise motion control.
Directly injecting both ego and other vehicle trajectory information into a diffusion transformer allows for fine-grained, multi-agent control over generated driving scenes.
Explicit control over surrounding agents is essential for generating realistic interactive scenarios and addressing corner cases in autonomous driving simulation.
originalProblem: Existing driving world models primarily focus on controlling only the ego vehicle, leading to unrealistic multi-agent interaction simulations.
The inability to precisely control other vehicles limits the generation of diverse and challenging driving scenarios crucial for autonomous driving system testing.
There is a modality mismatch between abstract Bird's Eye View (BEV) trajectory points and pixel-level video representations, complicating the integration of motion guidance.
results: The EOT-WM achieved state-of-the-art performance on the nuScenes dataset with an FID of 4.8 and FVD of 40.0, representing a 30% and 55% improvement over the previous best model, Vista.
Qualitative evaluations demonstrated enhanced action controllability, accurately depicting complex interactions like stationary roadside vehicles or backing trucks.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of Video-based Trajectory Representation (VTR), Aligned Motion Guidance Generation (AMGG), and the explicit inclusion of other vehicle trajectories.
solution: A Video-based Trajectory Representation (VTR) converts abstract Bird's Eye View (BEV) trajectories into visual "trajectory videos" to align with pixel-level video data.
An Aligned Motion Guidance Generation (AMGG) component uses a shared Spatial-Temporal Variational Auto Encoder (STVAE) to encode both scene videos and trajectory videos into a unified latent space.

A Trajectory-injected Diffusion Transformer (TiDiT) modifies a standard diffusion model to generate future video frames conditioned on initial frames, text, and the aligned ego-other vehicle trajectory latents.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Li Auto Inc. developed EOT-WM, a driving world model that unifies ego and other vehicle trajectories within a video latent space, enabling more realistic and controllable multi-agent driving simulations. The model achieved state-of-the-art performance, outperforming previous methods like Vista by 30% in FID and 55% in FVD, facilitating the generation of diverse and novel driving scenarios.

title: Other Vehicle Trajectories Are Also Needed: A Driving World Model Unifies Ego-Other Vehicle Trajectories in Video Latent Space

abstract:
Recent advancements in world models have revolutionized dynamic environment
simulation, allowing systems to foresee future states and assess potential
actions. In autonomous driving, these capabilities help vehicles anticipate the
behavior of other road users, perform risk-aware planning, accelerate training
in simulation, and adapt to novel scenarios, thereby enhancing safety and
reliability. Current approaches exhibit deficiencies in maintaining robust 3D
geometric consistency or accumulating artifacts during occlusion handling, both
critical for reliable safety assessment in autonomous navigation tasks. To
address this, we introduce GeoDrive, which explicitly integrates robust 3D
geometry conditions into driving world models to enhance spatial understanding
and action controllability. Specifically, we first extract a 3D representation
from the input frame and then obtain its 2D rendering based on the
user-specified ego-car trajectory. To enable dynamic modeling, we propose a
dynamic editing module during training to enhance the renderings by editing the
positions of the vehicles. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method
significantly outperforms existing models in both action accuracy and 3D
spatial awareness, leading to more realistic, adaptable, and reliable scene
modeling for safer autonomous driving. Additionally, our model can generalize
to novel trajectories and offers interactive scene editing capabilities, such
as object editing and object trajectory control.

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating 3D geometric priors into a generative world model is crucial for maintaining spatio-temporal coherence and avoiding physically implausible outcomes in dynamic driving scenes.
Translating numerical action commands into visually grounded conditioning inputs through dynamic rendering provides a substantially stronger and more stable control signal for generative models.

A lightweight condition encoder coupled with a frozen, pre-trained video diffusion backbone can efficiently refine geometrically accurate 3D renderings into photorealistic, controllable videos, achieving high performance with significantly less training data.
originalProblem: Existing driving world models lack robust 3D geometric consistency, leading to structural incoherence and physically implausible object interactions critical for autonomous driving safety.
Achieving precise and stable control over ego-vehicle trajectories and non-ego agent dynamics is challenging, often relying on dense annotations or weak numerical action conditioning.
Current models struggle to plausibly simulate the independent, kinematically constrained motion of dynamic objects, limiting their utility in complex, interactive driving scenarios.
results: GeoDrive significantly improves trajectory following accuracy, reducing Average Displacement Error (ADE) to 1.62 compared to 2.77 for baselines and achieving superior visual quality (e.g., FID 7.17 vs 8.35).
The model demonstrates remarkable data efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art performance with only 5 hours of training data, a 99.7% reduction compared to baselines requiring 1740 hours.

It enables practical applications such as precise object trajectory control for non-ego vehicles, intuitive scene editing (object removal/replacement), and serves as an interactive simulation environment for Visual Language Action (VLA) models to refine planning.
solution: GeoDrive extracts robust 3D representations from a single image using MonST3R, creating a point cloud that disentangles static scene geometry from moving objects.

It renders a video sequence based on the extracted 3D representation and user-specified camera trajectories, enhanced by a novel dynamic editing module that adjusts vehicle positions to simulate motion.

A dual-branch architecture employs a frozen latent video diffusion backbone (CogVideo) with a lightweight condition encoder, refining geometrically consistent rendered views into photorealistic, controllable videos.
paper_summary.summary: GeoDrive, developed by researchers from Peking University, Li Auto Inc., and UC Berkeley, is a 3D geometry-informed driving world model that generates photorealistic videos with precise action control. It integrates 3D scene understanding with a latent video diffusion model, achieving superior trajectory following with an Average Displacement Error of 1.62 and using 99.7% less training data than previous methods.

title: GeoDrive: 3D Geometry-Informed Driving World Model with Precise Action  Control

abstract: Vision-Language-Action models have shown great promise for autonomous driving, yet they suffer from degraded perception after unfreezing the visual encoder and struggle with accumulated instability in long-term planning. To address these challenges, we propose EvoDriveVLA-a novel collaborative perception-planning distillation framework that integrates self-anchored perceptual constraints and oracle-guided trajectory optimization. Specifically, self-anchored visual distillation leverages self-anchor teacher to deliver visual anchoring constraints, regularizing student representations via trajectory-guided key-region awareness. In parallel, oracle-guided trajectory distillation employs a future-aware oracle teacher with coarse-to-fine trajectory refinement and Monte Carlo dropout sampling to produce high-quality trajectory candidates, thereby selecting the optimal trajectory to guide the student's prediction. EvoDriveVLA achieves SOTA performance in open-loop evaluation and significantly enhances performance in closed-loop evaluation. Our code is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: A dual-focus distillation approach, simultaneously addressing visual perception robustness and planning stability, is crucial for advancing VLA models in autonomous driving.
Equipping a teacher model with privileged future information and advanced refinement strategies enables it to generate significantly superior and more reliable trajectories for student learning.

Preserving pre-trained visual representations through token-level self-anchored distillation while allowing task-specific adaptation effectively mitigates visual encoder degradation during fine-tuning.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving suffer from degraded visual perception when their pre-trained visual encoders are fine-tuned on driving-specific datasets.
VLA models exhibit instability and suboptimal performance in long-term trajectory planning due to accumulated errors.

Existing knowledge distillation methods for autonomous driving VLA models often lack superior teacher guidance, do not adequately manage visual encoder adaptation, and provide limited trajectory diversity for learning.
results: EvoDriveVLA achieved state-of-the-art open-loop performance on nuScenes, showing a 21-22% improvement in L2 error and a 40-60% reduction in collision rate compared to OpenDriveVLA.
In closed-loop evaluations on NAVSIM, the distilled 3B EvoDriveVLA model surpassed the base Qwen2.5-VL 3B by 3.4 PDMS points (4.2% increase) and larger 8B models by 2.0 PDMS points (2.4% increase).

Ablation studies confirmed that both oracle-guided trajectory distillation (reducing trajectory loss by shifting distributions and alleviating outliers) and self-anchored visual distillation contributed positively to prediction accuracy and robustness.
solution: A collaborative perception-planning distillation framework, EvoDriveVLA, is introduced, comprising self-anchored visual distillation and oracle-guided trajectory distillation.

Self-anchored visual distillation uses a frozen visual encoder copy (self-anchor teacher) and an AnchorFormer module to apply token-level constraints, preserving general-purpose visual representations during fine-tuning.

Oracle-guided trajectory distillation employs a future-aware oracle teacher with privileged future information, along with coarse-to-fine trajectory refinement and MC-Dropout sampling, to generate diverse, high-quality trajectory guidance.
paper_summary.summary: EvoDriveVLA, a collaborative distillation framework developed by Peking University and XPeng Motors, enhances autonomous driving Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by improving visual perception robustness and long-term trajectory planning stability. The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes and NAVSIM benchmarks, with a distilled 3B model outperforming larger 8B models in closed-loop evaluations.

title: EvoDriveVLA: Evolving Autonomous Driving Vision-Language-Action Model via Collaborative Perception-Planning Distillation

abstract: Recent successes in autoregressive (AR) generation models, such as the GPT series in natural language processing, have motivated efforts to replicate this success in visual tasks. Some works attempt to extend this approach to autonomous driving by building video-based world models capable of generating realistic future video sequences and predicting ego states. However, prior works tend to produce unsatisfactory results, as the classic GPT framework is designed to handle 1D contextual information, such as text, and lacks the inherent ability to model the spatial and temporal dynamics essential for video generation. In this paper, we present DrivingWorld, a GPT-style world model for autonomous driving, featuring several spatial-temporal fusion mechanisms. This design enables effective modeling of both spatial and temporal dynamics, facilitating high-fidelity, long-duration video generation. Specifically, we propose a next-state prediction strategy to model temporal coherence between consecutive frames and apply a next-token prediction strategy to capture spatial information within each frame. To further enhance generalization ability, we propose a novel masking strategy and reweighting strategy for token prediction to mitigate long-term drifting issues and enable precise control. Our work demonstrates the ability to produce high-fidelity and consistent video clips of over 40 seconds in duration, which is over 2 times longer than state-of-the-art driving world models. Experiments show that, in contrast to prior works, our method achieves superior visual quality and significantly more accurate controllable future video generation. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decoupling spatio-temporal processing into distinct temporal and multimodal attention layers within a GPT-style architecture significantly enhances efficiency and improves the quality of long-duration video generation for complex driving scenes.

Strategically corrupting training data through a random masking strategy effectively addresses the content drifting problem inherent in long-term autoregressive generation, leading to more robust and temporally consistent outputs.

Prioritizing ego-state information through balanced attention is crucial for maintaining precise control over generated trajectories in visually rich environments, enabling practical application in autonomous driving planning.
A temporal-aware VQVAE tokenizer, which explicitly models temporal dependencies, is essential for generating high-fidelity and temporally coherent video sequences compared to single-image VQGANs.
originalProblem: Vanilla GPT frameworks, designed for 1D text sequences, struggle to effectively model the complex spatial and temporal dynamics required for high-fidelity video generation in autonomous driving.
Existing driving world models often face limitations in the duration, visual fidelity, or precise controllability of generated predictions, typically producing much shorter video clips.
Autoregressive generation for long video sequences is prone to content drifting and error accumulation, leading to degradation over time and reduced robustness.
Autonomous driving systems require robust prediction for out-of-distribution and long-tail scenarios, which traditional data-driven models find challenging to cover comprehensively.
results: Successfully generates high-fidelity and temporally consistent driving videos exceeding 40 seconds (400 frames at 10 Hz) from just 15 conditioning frames, a substantial improvement over prior state-of-the-art models.

Achieves comparable state-of-the-art performance on the NuScenes dataset (FID: 7.4; FVD: 90.9) in zero-shot settings, demonstrating robust generalization and superior generation quality over much longer durations.

Ablation studies confirm the Random Masking Strategy (RMS) substantially mitigates content drifting, increasing FVD scores by 4% to 32% without it, and the architecture is significantly more memory-efficient than vanilla GPT-2, avoiding OOM errors for long sequences.
solution: A hybrid token prediction strategy with a Temporal-multimodal Fusion Module decouples spatio-temporal processing, using causal attention for temporal layers and bidirectional attention for multimodal layers within a frame.

A Temporal-aware Vector Quantized Tokenizer embeds self-attention layers before and after VQGAN quantization to capture temporal dependencies, alongside a relative vehicle pose tokenizer for continuous control.
Introduced a Random Masking Strategy (RMS) during training to randomly replace ground-truth tokens, mitigating content drifting and enhancing robustness during long-term autoregressive inference.
Utilized Balanced Attention by increasing attention weights for pose tokens, ensuring precise control over ego-vehicle trajectory despite the imbalance between image and pose tokens.
paper_summary.summary: DrivingWorld introduces a GPT-style world model for autonomous driving, employing a hybrid token prediction strategy and temporal-aware tokenizers to generate high-fidelity, controllable driving videos exceeding 40 seconds. The model achieves comparable state-of-the-art performance on public benchmarks while significantly extending generation duration and enhancing spatio-temporal consistency.

title: DrivingWorld: Constructing World Model for Autonomous Driving via Video GPT

abstract: Despite remarkable progress in driving world models, their potential for autonomous systems remains largely untapped: the world models are mostly learned for world simulation and decoupled from trajectory planning. While recent efforts aim to unify world modeling and planning in a single framework, the synergistic facilitation mechanism of world modeling for planning still requires further exploration. In this work, we introduce a new driving paradigm named Policy World Model (PWM), which not only integrates world modeling and trajectory planning within a unified architecture, but is also able to benefit planning using the learned world knowledge through the proposed action-free future state forecasting scheme. Through collaborative state-action prediction, PWM can mimic the human-like anticipatory perception, yielding more reliable planning performance. To facilitate the efficiency of video forecasting, we further introduce a dynamically enhanced parallel token generation mechanism, equipped with a context-guided tokenizer and an adaptive dynamic focal loss. Despite utilizing only front camera input, our method matches or exceeds state-of-the-art approaches that rely on multi-view and multi-modal inputs. Code and model weights will be released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Pre-training world modeling on action-free video data effectively learns robust environmental dynamics, which can be explicitly leveraged to enhance downstream trajectory planning through anticipatory perception.
A Dynamic Focal Loss improves the accuracy of future frame prediction by adaptively weighting changing tokens, leading to more realistic and temporally coherent forecasts.

Collaborative state-action prediction, where actions are conditioned on predicted future states, fundamentally redefines the planning process, yielding significantly safer driving policies by mimicking human-like foresight.
originalProblem: Existing autonomous driving systems largely decouple world models (for environmental simulation) from trajectory planning policy models.
Unified architectural approaches often treat world modeling (future frame prediction) and planning (action generation) as independent tasks, lacking explicit synergistic knowledge sharing.
Conventional action-conditioned video generation for world models relies heavily on costly labeled action data and multi-modal sensor setups (e.g., LiDAR), limiting scalability and efficiency.
results: Achieved a significantly reduced collision rate of 0.04% on the nuScenes dataset, outperforming state-of-the-art methods like Drive-OccWorld (0.11%) and DiffusionDrive (0.08%).
Attained a Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS) of 88.1 on the NAVSIM benchmark using only a single front camera, matching the performance of DiffusionDrive which uses both camera and LiDAR inputs.

Ablation studies confirmed the crucial impact of action-free video world knowledge pre-training and Dynamic Focal Loss on both future frame prediction quality and improved safety metrics (e.g., higher Time-to-Collision and No-At-Fault Collisions).
solution: Introduces the Policy World Model (PWM), a unified autoregressive Transformer that intrinsically links world modeling and trajectory planning.
Employs an efficient image tokenizer with context-guided compression to represent video frames compactly (28 tokens/frame) while maintaining visual coherence.

Utilizes action-free video generation for large-scale pre-training on unlabeled data and integrates Dynamic Focal Loss to prioritize dynamic regions, enabling collaborative state-action prediction during planning based on forecasted future states.
paper_summary.summary: The Policy World Model (PWM) from Dalian University of Technology integrates world modeling and trajectory planning within a unified autoregressive Transformer for autonomous driving. This system leverages action-free video generation for robust world dynamics and collaboratively predicts actions based on forecasted future states, achieving a 0.04% collision rate on nuScenes and comparable performance to LiDAR-based systems using only a single front camera on NAVSIM.

title: From Forecasting to Planning: Policy World Model for Collaborative State-Action Prediction

abstract:
World models that forecast environmental changes from actions are vital for
autonomous driving models with strong generalization. The prevailing driving
world model mainly build on video prediction model. Although these models can
produce high-fidelity video sequences with advanced diffusion-based generator,
they are constrained by their predictive duration and overall generalization
capabilities. In this paper, we explore to solve this problem by combining
generation loss with MAE-style feature-level context learning. In particular,
we instantiate this target with three key design: (1) A more scalable Diffusion
Transformer (DiT) structure trained with extra mask construction task. (2) we
devise diffusion-related mask tokens to deal with the fuzzy relations between
mask reconstruction and generative diffusion process. (3) we extend mask
construction task to spatial-temporal domain by utilizing row-wise mask for
shifted self-attention rather than masked self-attention in MAE. Then, we adopt
a row-wise cross-view module to align with this mask design. Based on above
improvement, we propose MaskGWM: a Generalizable driving World Model embodied
with Video Mask reconstruction. Our model contains two variants: MaskGWM-long,
focusing on long-horizon prediction, and MaskGWM-mview, dedicated to multi-view
generation. Comprehensive experiments on standard benchmarks validate the
effectiveness of the proposed method, which contain normal validation of
Nuscene dataset, long-horizon rollout of OpenDV-2K dataset and zero-shot
validation of Waymo dataset. Quantitative metrics on these datasets show our
method notably improving state-of-the-art driving world model.

keyInsights: Combining generative diffusion models with self-supervised mask reconstruction enhances the model's understanding of complex visual semantics, leading to more robust world models.

A sophisticated spatial-temporal masking strategy, including shared spatial masks and frame-specific row-wise temporal masks with shifted attention, is crucial for learning both spatial relationships and temporal dependencies effectively.
Using diffusion-related mask tokens, which adapt based on diffusion timestep and noise, helps the model balance global and local feature learning and improves performance.
originalProblem: Existing diffusion-based driving world models struggle with limited generalization, often performing poorly in unseen driving scenarios or environments with different visual characteristics.

The quality of video predictions from current models significantly degrades over longer time horizons due to accumulating errors, making them less reliable for long-term planning in autonomous driving.
Over-reliance on diffusion losses alone may not be sufficient for learning the complex visual semantics required for a robust and generalizable driving world model.
results: MaskGWM achieves state-of-the-art performance on the nuScenes dataset, with an FID of 9.5 and FVD of 163 for 2.5-second predictions, significantly outperforming previous methods like VISTA.
The model demonstrates superior generalization capabilities in zero-shot transfer experiments, successfully adapting to the visual characteristics of the unseen Waymo dataset without fine-tuning.

MaskGWM maintains high-quality and consistent predictions for significantly longer time horizons, up to 42 seconds, which is crucial for planning in autonomous driving, and can also generate synchronized multi-view predictions.
solution: SenseTime Research introduces MaskGWM, a novel driving world model that integrates a self-supervised video mask reconstruction task alongside the traditional diffusion process within a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture.

It employs a unique spatial-temporal masking strategy with two branches: a spatial branch using shared masks for spatial context and a temporal branch using frame-specific row-wise masks with shifted self-attention for temporal understanding.
MaskGWM utilizes diffusion-related mask tokens, initialized based on the diffusion timestep and noise level, allowing for adaptive balancing of global and local feature learning.
paper_summary.summary: MaskGWM, developed by SenseTime Research, is a driving world model that combines diffusion-based video generation with self-supervised mask reconstruction to improve generalization and long-horizon prediction. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes and Waymo datasets, demonstrating accurate video predictions up to 42 seconds and strong zero-shot generalization capabilities.

title: MaskGWM: A Generalizable Driving World Model with Video Mask  Reconstruction

abstract: Embodied navigation in open, dynamic environments demands accurate foresight of how the world will evolve and how actions will unfold over time. We propose AstraNav-World, an end-to-end world model that jointly reasons about future visual states and action sequences within a unified probabilistic framework. Our framework integrates a diffusion-based video generator with a vision-language policy, enabling synchronized rollouts where predicted scenes and planned actions are updated simultaneously. Training optimizes two complementary objectives: generating action-conditioned multi-step visual predictions and deriving trajectories conditioned on those predicted visuals. This bidirectional constraint makes visual predictions executable and keeps decisions grounded in physically consistent, task-relevant futures, mitigating cumulative errors common in decoupled "envision-then-plan" pipelines. Experiments across diverse embodied navigation benchmarks show improved trajectory accuracy and higher success rates. Ablations confirm the necessity of tight vision-action coupling and unified training, with either branch removal degrading both prediction quality and policy reliability. In real-world testing, AstraNav-World demonstrated exceptional zero-shot capabilities, adapting to previously unseen scenarios without any real-world fine-tuning. These results suggest that AstraNav-World captures transferable spatial understanding and planning-relevant navigation dynamics, rather than merely overfitting to simulation-specific data distribution. Overall, by unifying foresight vision and control within a single generative model, we move closer to reliable, interpretable, and general-purpose embodied agents that operate robustly in open-ended real-world settings.

keyInsights: Tightly coupling future vision prediction and action planning within a single probabilistic framework effectively mitigates inconsistencies and error accumulation inherent in decoupled approaches.
Explicit prediction of future visual observations by a diffusion-based video generator provides critical guidance for policy planning, enhancing navigation accuracy.
The model demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization from simulation to real-world environments, indicating it learns transferable spatial understanding and planning-relevant dynamics.
