originalProblem: Existing embodied navigation methods suffer from decoupled "envision-then-plan" paradigms, leading to accumulated physical uncertainty and inconsistencies between predicted futures and planned actions.
Lack of robust modeling for physical laws and temporal dynamics in complex, open-ended environments hinders reliable autonomous navigation.
Prior world models and VLA-like policies often operate sequentially, limiting the intrinsic consistency between visual foresight and actionable plans.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art success rates on R2R-CE (67.9% SR), RxR-CE (72.9% SR), and HM3D-OVON (45.7% SR), with the Diffusion Policy variant consistently outperforming prior methods.
Exhibited exceptional zero-shot transfer to a physical robot, successfully executing navigation tasks in unseen real-world environments without any real-world training or fine-tuning.

Ablation studies confirm the necessity of the video generator for improved navigation performance, and Sparse Foresight Scheduling provides up to 6.7x faster inference with minimal performance impact.
solution: A unified generative framework integrates a Vision-Language Model (VLM) planner, a VLM-conditioned diffusion-based video generator, and an action policy head, allowing for joint reasoning of future states and actions.

Bidirectional constraints are established between the predicted scenes and planned actions, particularly through Multimodal Fusion Cross-Attention (MMFCA) in the Diffusion Policy, ensuring visual predictions are executable and actions are grounded.
Sparse Foresight Scheduling (SFS) is implemented to optimize computational efficiency, enabling sparse joint predictions of future frames and actions while maintaining performance.
paper_summary.summary: AstraNav-World introduces a unified generative world model that tightly couples future vision prediction with action planning, improving consistency and robustness in embodied navigation. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on R2R-CE, RxR-CE, and HM3D-OVON benchmarks, demonstrating significant zero-shot transfer capabilities to a physical robot in unseen real-world environments.

title: AstraNav-World: World Model for Foresight Control and Consistency

abstract: World models have become central to autonomous driving, where accurate scene understanding and future prediction are crucial for safe control. Recent work has explored using vision-language models (VLMs) for planning, yet existing approaches typically treat perception, prediction, and planning as separate modules. We propose UniDrive-WM, a unified VLM-based world model that jointly performs driving-scene understanding, trajectory planning, and trajectory-conditioned future image generation within a single architecture. UniDrive-WM's trajectory planner predicts a future trajectory, which conditions a VLM-based image generator to produce plausible future frames. These predictions provide additional supervisory signals that enhance scene understanding and iteratively refine trajectory generation. We further compare discrete and continuous output representations for future image prediction, analyzing their influence on downstream driving performance. Experiments on the challenging Bench2Drive benchmark show that UniDrive-WM produces high-fidelity future images and improves planning performance by 7.3% in L2 trajectory error and 10.4% in collision rate over the previous best method. These results demonstrate the advantages of tightly integrating VLM-driven reasoning, planning, and generative world modeling for autonomous driving. The project page is available at this https URL.
title: UniDrive-WM: Unified Understanding, Planning and Generation World Model For Autonomous Driving

abstract: World models have demonstrated significant promise for data synthesis in autonomous driving. However, existing methods predominantly concentrate on single-modality generation, typically focusing on either multi-camera video or LiDAR sequence synthesis. In this paper, we propose UniDriveDreamer, a single-stage unified multimodal world model for autonomous driving, which directly generates multimodal future observations without relying on intermediate representations or cascaded modules. Our framework introduces a LiDAR-specific variational autoencoder (VAE) designed to encode input LiDAR sequences, alongside a video VAE for multi-camera images. To ensure cross-modal compatibility and training stability, we propose Unified Latent Anchoring (ULA), which explicitly aligns the latent distributions of the two modalities. The aligned features are fused and processed by a diffusion transformer that jointly models their geometric correspondence and temporal evolution. Additionally, structured scene layout information is projected per modality as a conditioning signal to guide the synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniDriveDreamer outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in both video and LiDAR generation, while also yielding measurable improvements in downstream

keyInsights: A single-stage architecture with deep fusion through a Diffusion Transformer yields superior spatiotemporal and cross-modal consistency compared to cascaded multimodal generation.
Unified Latent Anchoring (ULA) effectively aligns the latent spaces of distinct modalities (camera and LiDAR), stabilizing training and ensuring cross-modal compatibility.
Transforming 3D LiDAR point clouds into 2D range images, with row repetition, addresses data sparsity and enhances reconstruction fidelity for VAE-based processing.
originalProblem: High costs associated with acquiring and annotating vast amounts of real-world multi-sensor data for autonomous driving system development.

Existing world models for autonomous driving typically generate data for a single modality (camera or LiDAR) or use decoupled/cascaded architectures for multimodal synthesis, leading to poor cross-modal consistency.
Autonomous vehicles require a holistic, multi-sensor understanding of their environment, necessitating coherent and synchronized multimodal data for training.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art generation quality, with FID 2.81 and FVD 11.44 for video, and MMD 0.27 and JSD 0.039 for LiDAR, outperforming previous multimodal methods by a wide margin.
Improved downstream 3D object detection performance by +0.8 mAP and +0.52 NDS when UniDriveDreamer's synthetic data augmented BEVFusion training.
Demonstrated high qualitative fidelity with sharper camera outputs, more complete LiDAR ground coverage, and robust spatiotemporal and cross-modal alignment across diverse scenarios.
solution: Developed UniDriveDreamer, a single-stage unified multimodal world model that deeply fuses multi-camera video and LiDAR data within a shared latent space.
Utilized modality-specific Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) for camera and LiDAR data, with a novel Unified Latent Anchoring (ULA) module to align their latent distributions.
Employed a Diffusion Transformer for joint generation, enabling bidirectional interaction and strong conditioning from structured scene layouts and textual prompts via flow matching optimization.
paper_summary.summary: UniDriveDreamer introduces a single-stage multimodal world model that directly generates temporally consistent multi-camera videos and LiDAR sweeps for autonomous driving. This model achieves state-of-the-art generation quality and measurably boosts downstream 3D object detection performance by augmenting training data.

title: UniDriveDreamer: A Single-Stage Multimodal World Model for Autonomous Driving

abstract: World model-based searching and planning are widely recognized as a promising path toward human-level physical intelligence. However, current driving world models primarily rely on video diffusion models, which specialize in visual generation but lack the flexibility to incorporate other modalities like action. In contrast, autoregressive transformers have demonstrated exceptional capability in modeling multimodal data. Our work aims to unify both driving model simulation and trajectory planning into a single sequence modeling problem. We introduce a multimodal driving language based on interleaved image and action tokens, and develop DrivingGPT to learn joint world modeling and planning through standard next-token prediction. Our DrivingGPT demonstrates strong performance in both action-conditioned video generation and end-to-end planning, outperforming strong baselines on large-scale nuPlan and NAVSIM benchmarks.

organization_info.name: Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics, HKISI, CAS

keyInsights: Autoregressive transformers, by treating driving data as a unified multi-modal sequence, are well-suited for both world modeling and end-to-end planning in autonomous driving.
Applying positional embeddings to action tokens is critical for the transformer to effectively learn planning behaviors.
Data quality and curation are more important than raw data quantity for training robust planning capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing driving world models, primarily diffusion-based, struggle with seamless multi-modal integration (especially actions) and differentiable planning.
Traditional autonomous driving pipelines are modular, leading to complex systems where perception, prediction, and planning are handled separately.
Diffusion models face challenges in maintaining long-term temporal consistency and generating long-duration videos in dynamic driving scenarios.
results: DrivingGPT significantly outperforms diffusion-based baselines like SVD and CogvideoX in action-conditioned video generation, achieving lower FVD (142.61 vs. SVD's 227.54) and FID (12.78 vs. SVD's 24.03) on the NAVSIM dataset.
The model demonstrates strong end-to-end planning performance on the NAVSIM benchmark, achieving a Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS) of 82.4%, surpassing a ResNet-50 + MLP baseline (77.8%).

DrivingGPT can generate temporally coherent driving videos up to 64 frames (32 seconds) without significant quality degradation, showcasing its superior long-term consistency and reduced object hallucination compared to diffusion models.
solution: Formulates driving world modeling and end-to-end planning as a unified 'next-token prediction' problem using a Llama-like autoregressive transformer.
Develops a novel 'multi-modal driving language' by tokenizing continuous camera observations via VQ-VAE and discrete frame-to-frame relative actions into a single interleaved sequence.
Trains the transformer on large-scale driving datasets (nuPlan, NAVSIM) using a standard cross-entropy loss to implicitly learn both future observations and optimal actions.
paper_summary.summary: This research unifies driving world modeling and planning into a single framework using multi-modal autoregressive transformers. The proposed DrivingGPT leverages a novel 'driving language' to convert visual observations and relative actions into discrete tokens, demonstrating strong performance in action-conditioned video generation and end-to-end trajectory planning.

title: DrivingGPT: Unifying Driving World Modeling and Planning with Multi-modal Autoregressive Transformers

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are emerging as highly effective planning models for end-to-end autonomous driving systems. However, current works mostly rely on imitation learning from sparse trajectory annotations and under-utilize their potential as generative models. We propose Generative Scenario Rollouts (GeRo), a plug-and-play framework for VLA models that jointly performs planning and generation of language-grounded future traffic scenes through an autoregressive rollout strategy. First, a VLA model is trained to encode ego vehicle and agent dynamics into latent tokens under supervision from planning, motion, and language tasks, facilitating text-aligned generation. Next, GeRo performs language-conditioned autoregressive generation. Given multi-view images, a scenario description, and ego-action questions, it generates future latent tokens and textual responses to guide long-horizon rollouts. A rollout-consistency loss stabilizes predictions using ground truth or pseudo-labels, mitigating drift and preserving text-action alignment. This design enables GeRo to perform temporally consistent, language-grounded rollouts that support long-horizon reasoning and multi-agent planning. On Bench2Drive, GeRo improves driving score and success rate by +15.7 and +26.2, respectively. By integrating reinforcement learning with generative rollouts, GeRo achieves state-of-the-art closed-loop and open-loop performance, demonstrating strong zero-shot robustness. These results highlight the promise of generative, language-conditioned reasoning as a foundation for safer and more interpretable end-to-end autonomous driving.

keyInsights: Harnessing the generative potential of VLA models through autoregressive scenario rollouts enables proactive, reasoning-capable planning, offering greater robustness in novel or ambiguous driving situations.

Explicitly enforcing language-action alignment and incorporating safety-critical reinforcement learning rewards significantly improves both the safety and interpretability of autonomous driving systems.

A modular framework combining language-grounded pretraining, consistency-guided generation, and RL fine-tuning results in exceptional cross-dataset generalization and zero-shot robustness, crucial for real-world applicability.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving suffer from sparse language-action supervision, making them brittle in complex situations and limiting procedural planning.
Current VLA methods largely underutilize their generative capabilities, primarily acting as discriminative planners through imitation learning from ground-truth trajectories.
Language supervision often describes 'what' is happening rather than 'how' actions unfold, leading to a disconnect between linguistic instructions and actual vehicle behaviors.
results: Achieved new state-of-the-art closed-loop performance on the Bench2Drive benchmark, with GeRo (Qwen2.5VL) improving the Driving Score by +15.7 and Success Rate by +26.2% over its baseline.
Demonstrated significant enhancements in multi-ability urban driving tasks, with GeRo (Qwen2.5VL) showing a 140% improvement in mean ability across complex scenarios like merging and overtaking.
Exhibited strong zero-shot robustness by reducing average L2 trajectory error by 53.1% and collision rate by 58.3% on the nuScenes dataset, despite being trained solely on Bench2Drive.
solution: GeRo introduces a plug-and-play framework that extends VLA models to autoregressively generate future scenarios, encompassing ego-vehicle trajectories, multi-agent motions, and corresponding language descriptions.

The framework employs a two-stage methodology: pretraining with multi-task supervision (planning, motion prediction, VLA) followed by generative scenario rollouts that ensure temporal consistency using a novel rollout-consistency loss.

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is integrated via Generalized Rollout Policy Optimization (GRPO) with differentiable surrogate rewards for safety (collision, time-to-collision) and language alignment, refining behavior beyond imitation learning.
paper_summary.summary: Qualcomm AI Research developed the Generative Scenario Rollouts (GeRo) framework, which enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving by enabling autoregressive, language-conditioned generation of future scenarios and actions. This approach led to new state-of-the-art closed-loop driving performance on the Bench2Drive benchmark, including a +26.2% success rate improvement, and demonstrated strong zero-shot robustness on nuScenes.

title: Generative Scenario Rollouts for End-to-End Autonomous Driving

abstract: Physics-aware driving world model is essential for drive planning, out-of-distribution data synthesis, and closed-loop evaluation. However, existing methods often rely on a single diffusion model to directly map driving actions to videos, which makes learning difficult and leads to physically inconsistent outputs. To overcome these challenges, we propose GenieDrive, a novel framework designed for physics-aware driving video generation. Our approach starts by generating 4D occupancy, which serves as a physics-informed foundation for subsequent video generation. 4D occupancy contains rich physical information, including high-resolution 3D structures and dynamics. To facilitate effective compression of such high-resolution occupancy, we propose a VAE that encodes occupancy into a latent tri-plane representation, reducing the latent size to only 58% of that used in previous methods. We further introduce Mutual Control Attention (MCA) to accurately model the influence of control on occupancy evolution, and we jointly train the VAE and the subsequent prediction module in an end-to-end manner to maximize forecasting accuracy. Together, these designs yield a 7.2% improvement in forecasting mIoU at an inference speed of 41 FPS, while using only 3.47 M parameters. Additionally, a Normalized Multi-View Attention is introduced in the video generation model to generate multi-view driving videos with guidance from our 4D occupancy, significantly improving video quality with a 20.7% reduction in FVD. Experiments demonstrate that GenieDrive enables highly controllable, multi-view consistent, and physics-aware driving video generation.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: End-to-end training of the VAE and prediction module is highly effective for continuous latent representations, aligning the latent space with the forecasting task, unlike discrete or diffusion-based methods.
Mutual Control Attention (MCA) is critical for robustly modeling the dynamic interaction between occupancy evolution and explicit driving control signals.

A normalization strategy within the Normalized Multi-View Attention (MVA) module is essential for stably integrating multi-view consistency into a pretrained video generation model during fine-tuning.
originalProblem: Existing video-based driving world models, often black boxes, produce physically inconsistent or implausible outputs due to overfitting training data biases.
Efficiently and accurately generating high-resolution 4D occupancy for driving scenarios is challenging, frequently incurring high computational demands or leading to lossy representations.
A lack of truly controllable and multi-view consistent long driving video generation hinders advanced applications like drive planning and out-of-distribution data synthesis.
results: Achieved a 20.7% reduction in Frechet Video Distance (FVD) for video generation, demonstrating higher quality and better alignment with real video distribution.
Improved 4D occupancy forecasting mIoU by 7.2% and IoU by 4% compared to prior state-of-the-art, while maintaining robust accuracy over longer prediction horizons.
The model is highly efficient, with a lightweight 3.47M parameter occupancy model achieving 41 FPS inference, and supports editable driving data synthesis and sim-to-real scenario generation.
solution: A two-stage pipeline is introduced: a lightweight occupancy world model first predicts future 4D occupancy, which then serves as physical guidance for multi-view video generation.

The lightweight occupancy world model employs a compact latent tri-plane representation, Mutual Control Attention (MCA) for robust next-occupancy prediction, and is trained end-to-end for representation alignment.

Generated 4D occupancy is converted into multi-view semantic maps via splatting, conditioning a fine-tuned video generation model enhanced by Normalized Multi-View Attention (MVA) for stable multi-view consistency.
paper_summary.summary: A physics-aware driving world model, GenieDrive, generates controllable, multi-view consistent, and long driving videos by using 4D occupancy as an intermediate physical representation. It reduces Frechet Video Distance by 20.7% and improves 4D occupancy forecasting mIoU by 7.2% compared to prior methods.

title: GenieDrive: Towards Physics-Aware Driving World Model with 4D Occupancy Guided Video Generation

abstract: The autonomous driving community is increasingly focused on addressing the challenges posed by out-of-distribution (OOD) driving scenarios. A dominant research trend seeks to enhance end-to-end (E2E) driving systems by integrating vision-language models (VLMs), leveraging their rich world knowledge and reasoning abilities to improve generalization across diverse environments. However, most existing VLMs or vision-language agents (VLAs) are built upon autoregressive (AR) models. In this paper, we observe that existing AR-based VLMs -- limited by causal attention and sequential token generation -- often fail to maintain consistency and controllability between high-level reasoning and low-level planning. In contrast, recent discrete diffusion VLMs equipped with bidirectional attention exhibit superior controllability and reliability through iterative denoising. Building on these observations, we introduce dVLM-AD, a diffusion-based vision-language model that unifies perception, structured reasoning, and low-level planning for end-to-end driving. Evaluated on nuScenes and WOD-E2E, dVLM-AD yields more consistent reasoning-action pairs and achieves planning performance comparable to existing driving VLM/VLA systems despite a modest backbone, outperforming AR-based baselines with a 9 percent improvement in behavior-trajectory consistency and a 6 percent increase in RFS on long-tail WOD-E2E scenarios. These results suggest a controllable and reliable pathway for scalable end-to-end driving.

keyInsights: Discrete diffusion models offer a principled alternative to autoregressive models for safety-critical applications like autonomous driving by inherently promoting reasoning-action consistency through iterative, bidirectional refinement.
Constrained infilling, achieved by anchoring structured templates during the denoising process, provides robust control over the model's reasoning flow, making it resilient to external manipulations.

Dynamically adjusting output length within structured slots, using mechanisms like a 'reduce token,' is essential for diffusion models to generate semantically accurate and variable-length text, overcoming limitations of fixed-slot approaches.
originalProblem: Existing autoregressive (AR) Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for autonomous driving often suffer from reasoning-action inconsistency, where high-level reasoning can be semantically misaligned with generated trajectories.
AR models are vulnerable to prompt manipulation, allowing users to bypass safety-critical reasoning steps and leading to uncontrollable or unsafe behaviors.
Fixed-length mask windows in structured generation, common in some diffusion model applications, can lead to a "slot-length bias" where output is based on length rather than semantic appropriateness.
results: dVLM-AD achieves superior consistency, with 98.6% Object Explanation and 87.8% Longitudinal Behavior Trajectory consistency on nuScenes, significantly outperforming AR baselines.

The model demonstrates strong robustness against prompt perturbations, maintaining 0.00% "Structured Reasoning Bypassed" and minimal performance degradation, unlike AR models that show significant drops in RFS and consistency.

It achieves the highest Rater Feedback Score (7.633) and lowest Average Displacement Error (1.285m at 3 seconds) on the challenging WOD-E2E test set, outperforming leading AR-based methods despite using a more modest backbone.
solution: The dVLM-AD framework uses a discrete diffusion Vision-Language Model (dVLM) as its backbone, leveraging its bidirectional attention for globally consistent reasoning and action generation.

It employs a structured Chain-of-Thought (CoT) template and template-anchored decoding, where only specific 'slots' are masked, enforcing robust and controllable reasoning against prompt perturbations.

A dynamic denoise strategy with a special 'reduce token' is introduced to adaptively shorten generated spans, addressing the slot-length bias by allowing variable-length outputs within a fixed template.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, NVIDIA, Stanford University, and Johns Hopkins University developed dVLM-AD, a diffusion-based Vision-Language Model for autonomous driving. This model improves the consistency between high-level reasoning and low-level planning while providing strong robustness against prompt manipulation, achieving top performance on the WOD-E2E benchmark.

title: dVLM-AD: Enhance Diffusion Vision-Language-Model for Driving via Controllable Reasoning

abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving methods aim to directly map raw sensor inputs to future driving actions such as planned trajectories, bypassing traditional modular pipelines. While these approaches have shown promise, they often operate under a one-shot paradigm that relies heavily on the current scene context, potentially underestimating the importance of scene dynamics and their temporal evolution. This limitation restricts the model's ability to make informed and adaptive decisions in complex driving scenarios. We propose a new perspective: the future trajectory of an autonomous vehicle is closely intertwined with the evolving dynamics of its environment, and conversely, the vehicle's own future states can influence how the surrounding scene unfolds. Motivated by this bidirectional relationship, we introduce SeerDrive, a novel end-to-end framework that jointly models future scene evolution and trajectory planning in a closed-loop manner. Our method first predicts future bird's-eye view (BEV) representations to anticipate the dynamics of the surrounding scene, then leverages this foresight to generate future-context-aware trajectories. Two key components enable this: (1) future-aware planning, which injects predicted BEV features into the trajectory planner, and (2) iterative scene modeling and vehicle planning, which refines both future scene prediction and trajectory generation through collaborative optimization. Extensive experiments on the NAVSIM and nuScenes benchmarks show that SeerDrive significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling the bidirectional interaction between an autonomous vehicle's actions and the dynamic evolution of the driving scene leads to more coherent and adaptive planning.
An iterative refinement process between a world model and a planning network allows for deeper integration of future foresight into decision-making, improving temporal awareness.
Decoupling the planning network's interaction with current and future scene contexts, then fusing the resulting ego features, effectively combines diverse contextual information.
originalProblem: Existing end-to-end autonomous driving systems primarily rely on a "one-shot" planning paradigm, which often underestimates the dynamic evolution of the driving environment.
Current methods largely neglect the bidirectional influence between the ego vehicle's planned actions and how surrounding agents and the scene will unfold.
The lack of deep, integrated future context in planning can lead to sub-optimal and less adaptive driving decisions in complex traffic scenarios.
results: Achieved a new state-of-the-art PDM Score of 90.7 on the NAVSIM dataset (using V2-99 backbone), outperforming prior methods like Hydra-NeXt (88.6) and DiffusionDrive (88.1).
Secured the lowest average L2 Displacement Error of 0.43m and a 0.06% collision rate on the nuScenes validation split, surpassing SparseDrive and MomAD.

Demonstrated superior performance on the challenging Bench2Drive dataset with the lowest average L2 Error (0.66), highest Driving Score (58.32), and Success Rate (30.17%), while maintaining an efficient 24 ms inference time.
solution: SeerDrive proposes a closed-loop, iterative framework that jointly refines future Bird's-Eye View (BEV) scene predictions and ego vehicle trajectory planning over multiple steps.
It employs a BEV World Modeling Network to anticipate scene dynamics and an End-to-End Planning Network that integrates both current and predicted future BEV features.
A decoupled planning strategy and Motion-aware Layer Normalization are used to effectively fuse current and future ego representations, enabling future-context-aware trajectory generation.
paper_summary.summary: SeerDrive introduces an end-to-end autonomous driving framework that iteratively and bidirectionally models the relationship between future scene evolution and ego vehicle trajectory planning. The system achieves a PDM Score of 90.7 on the NAVSIM dataset and an average L2 Displacement Error of 0.43m on nuScenes, demonstrating improved future-aware decision-making compared to existing methods.

title: Future-Aware End-to-End Driving: Bidirectional Modeling of Trajectory Planning and Scene Evolution

abstract: Achieving reliable and efficient planning in complex driving environments requires a model that can reason over the scene's geometry, appearance, and dynamics. We present UniDWM, a unified driving world model that advances autonomous driving through multifaceted representation learning. UniDWM constructs a structure- and dynamic-aware latent world representation that serves as a physically grounded state space, enabling consistent reasoning across perception, prediction, and planning. Specifically, a joint reconstruction pathway learns to recover the scene's structure, including geometry and visual texture, while a collaborative generation framework leverages a conditional diffusion transformer to forecast future world evolution within the latent space. Furthermore, we show that our UniDWM can be deemed as a variation of VAE, which provides theoretical guidance for the multifaceted representation learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of UniDWM in trajectory planning, 4D reconstruction and generation, highlighting the potential of multifaceted world representations as a foundation for unified driving intelligence. The code will be publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Framing the world model learning as a VAE/InfoVAE variant provides a principled theoretical foundation, guiding the learning of informative and coherent latent representations while mitigating posterior collapse.

Learning a multifaceted representation through joint reconstruction of 3D geometry, visual appearance, and ego motion provides mutually reinforcing supervisory signals, leading to a physically grounded and structure-aware latent space.
A self-supervised approach can effectively reduce dependence on expensive perception annotations, addressing a critical bottleneck in the scalability and deployment of autonomous driving systems.
originalProblem: End-to-end autonomous driving systems often suffer from error accumulation and heavy reliance on costly perception annotations, hindering scalability and practical deployment.
Existing world models often lack a unified latent representation that simultaneously captures 3D geometry, visual appearance, and scene dynamics with spatiotemporal coherence.
Many autonomous driving models rely on heuristic designs without a strong theoretical foundation, limiting systematic improvement and generalization.
results: UniDWM achieved the best trajectory planning performance among all label-free methods on the NAVSIM `navtest` split, outperforming prior approaches across all metrics (e.g., NC, DAC, EP, TTC, PDMS).
The model set new state-of-the-art results in 4D reconstruction on NAVSIM, recording the lowest Overall Chamfer Distance (1.727), Accuracy (1.878), and Completeness (1.575).

Finetuning UniDWM with the GRPO policy optimization algorithm further enhanced trajectory planning, demonstrating that its high-quality latent world model serves as a strong foundation for advanced control strategies.
solution: UniDWM frames the problem as a self-supervised learning task, theoretically grounded in a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and InfoVAE variant, to learn a comprehensive latent world representation.
A spatiotemporal encoder processes sequential visual observations and ego status into a unified latent embedding, while decoupled decoders reconstruct 3D geometry, visual appearance, and ego motion.

A collaborative generation framework, utilizing a Conditional Diffusion Transformer, extends the model to forecast future latent world representations, enabling robust perception, prediction, and planning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Sun Yat-sen University and Xpeng Motors developed UniDWM, a unified driving world model that learns a multifaceted, physically grounded latent representation from visual observations without requiring extensive perception annotations. This self-supervised approach achieves superior label-free trajectory planning performance and state-of-the-art 4D reconstruction on the NAVSIM dataset.

title: UniDWM: Towards a Unified Driving World Model via Multifaceted Representation Learning

abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving aims to generate safe and plausible planning policies from raw sensor input. Driving world models have shown great potential in learning rich representations by predicting the future evolution of a driving scene. However, existing driving world models primarily focus on visual scene representation, and motion representation is not explicitly designed to be planner-shared and inheritable, leaving a schism between the optimization of visual scene generation and the requirements of precise motion planning. We present WorldDrive, a holistic framework that couples scene generation and real-time planning via unifying vision and motion representation. We first introduce a Trajectory-aware Driving World Model, which conditions on a trajectory vocabulary to enforce consistency between visual dynamics and motion intentions, enabling the generation of diverse and plausible future scenes conditioned on a specific trajectory. We transfer the vision and motion encoders to a downstream Multi-modal Planner, ensuring the driving policy operates on mature representations pre-optimized by scene generation. A simple interaction between motion representation, visual representation, and ego status can generate high-quality, multi-modal trajectories. Furthermore, to exploit the world model's foresight, we propose a Future-aware Rewarder, which distills future latent representation from the frozen world model to evaluate and select optimal trajectories in real-time. Extensive experiments on the NAVSIM, NAVSIM-v2, and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate that WorldDrive achieves leading planning performance among vision-only methods while maintaining high-fidelity action-controlled video generation capabilities, providing strong evidence for the effectiveness of unifying vision and motion representation for robust autonomous driving.

keyInsights: Unifying vision and motion representations allows the planning module to directly inherit and leverage the rich dynamic understanding and motion priors learned by the generative world model.

Conditioning scene generation on a structured trajectory vocabulary enforces consistency between visual dynamics and specific motion intentions, leading to more controllable and plausible scene predictions.

Distilling planning-relevant future latent representations via a lightweight Future-aware Rewarder (FAR) significantly reduces computational latency, making predictive foresight from world models feasible for real-time autonomous driving.
originalProblem: Existing Driving World Models (DWMs) primarily emphasize visual scene representation, leading to a "representational schism" where motion understanding is not explicitly designed for planner-sharing.
Integrating DWMs with motion planning often incurs high computational costs and latency for generating future scenes, or sacrifices interpretability with latent-space planning.

A fundamental misalignment exists between scene simulators optimized for perceptual reconstruction and planners trained in isolation for action regression, preventing effective transfer of dynamic understanding.
results: WorldDrive achieves a Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS) of 89.0 on the NAVSIM navtrain split and an Extended PDMS (EPDMS) of 62.8 on the NAVSIM-v2 navhard split, surpassing various single-view and multi-view baselines.

The framework demonstrates low inference latency of 53ms on a single NVIDIA A800 GPU, significantly faster than other DWM-based planners (e.g., PWM at 570-850ms), by avoiding explicit future scene generation during inference.

It maintains high-fidelity, action-controlled video generation, achieving competitive FID of 12.8 and FVD of 131.7 on nuScenes, confirming realistic future scene synthesis consistent with specified motion conditions.
solution: WorldDrive introduces a holistic framework that unifies vision and motion representations, directly coupling scene generation with real-time planning by ensuring latent features for generation also guide actions.
A Trajectory-aware Driving World Model (TA-DWM) learns robust scene dynamics conditioned on a structured trajectory vocabulary and pre-optimizes vision and motion encoders.

A Multi-modal Trajectory Planner leverages inherited representations from TA-DWM, and a Future-aware Rewarder (FAR) distills planning-relevant future latents from the frozen world model for efficient, real-time trajectory evaluation without explicit video generation.
paper_summary.summary: WorldDrive unifies vision and motion representations to bridge the gap between scene generation and real-time planning in autonomous driving, achieving high planning performance by leveraging a trajectory-aware world model and a future-aware rewarder for efficient trajectory evaluation. The framework demonstrates competitive planning scores on NAVSIM benchmarks, outperforming several vision-only and multi-view baselines, while also generating high-fidelity, motion-controlled future scenes with low inference latency.

title: Bridging Scene Generation and Planning: Driving with World Model via Unifying Vision and Motion Representation

abstract: Autonomous driving is a complex and challenging task that aims at safe motion planning through scene understanding and reasoning. While vision-only autonomous driving methods have recently achieved notable performance, through enhanced scene understanding, several key issues, including lack of reasoning, low generalization performance and long-tail scenarios, still need to be addressed. In this paper, we present VLP, a novel Vision-Language-Planning framework that exploits language models to bridge the gap between linguistic understanding and autonomous driving. VLP enhances autonomous driving systems by strengthening both the source memory foundation and the self-driving car's contextual understanding. VLP achieves state-of-the-art end-to-end planning performance on the challenging NuScenes dataset by achieving 35.9\% and 60.5\% reduction in terms of average L2 error and collision rates, respectively, compared to the previous best method. Moreover, VLP shows improved performance in challenging long-tail scenarios and strong generalization capabilities when faced with new urban environments.

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LMs) can be integrated into end-to-end autonomous driving systems during training to infuse common-sense reasoning and human-like understanding, improving generalization.

Aligning visual Bird's Eye View (BEV) features and planning queries with LM-generated linguistic expectations via contrastive learning enhances both local semantic representation and global contextual awareness.

This language-guided training approach yields substantial performance gains across multiple autonomous driving tasks, including planning, perception, and prediction, without adding any computational overhead during inference.
originalProblem: Vision-only end-to-end autonomous driving systems often lack common-sense reasoning, struggling with complex or ambiguous driving situations.
These systems exhibit limited generalization performance, failing to maintain accuracy in out-of-distribution data such as new cities or infrequent "long-tail" scenarios.
Current methods struggle to bridge the gap between visual scene understanding and human-like cognitive interpretation required for robust decision-making.
results: VLP-VAD reduced average L2 error by 35.9% and collision rates by 60.5% in open-loop planning compared to the baseline VAD.
The framework demonstrated robust zero-shot generalization to new cities, with VLP-VAD reducing L2 error by up to 19.2% and collision rates by up to 48.7% when tested on unseen urban environments.

VLP consistently improved performance across various tasks, including Multi-Object Tracking (better AMOTA), Online Mapping (increased IoU for map elements), Motion Forecasting (reduced minADE/minFDE), and 3D Object Detection (improved NDS, including a 15.9% mAP gain in long-tail cases).
solution: VLP integrates language models into the perception, prediction, and planning (P3) pipeline of vision-based end-to-end autonomous driving systems, active only during training.

It introduces an Agent-centric Learning Paradigm (ALP) that uses contrastive learning to align region-of-interest BEV features with LM-generated 'agent expectation features' derived from ground-truth linguistic descriptions.

A Self-driving-car-centric Learning Paradigm (SLP) refines the ego-vehicle query by aligning it with an LM-generated 'ego-vehicle planning feature' that embeds human driving logic and ground-truth trajectories via a sample-wise contrastive loss.
paper_summary.summary: VLP (Vision Language Planning) integrates large language models into vision-based end-to-end autonomous driving systems during training to enhance common-sense reasoning and improve generalization. This approach led to a 35.9% reduction in planning L2 error and a 60.5% reduction in collision rates on the nuScenes dataset, while demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization to new cities and long-tail scenarios without increasing inference time.

title: VLP: Vision Language Planning for Autonomous Driving

abstract:
World models, especially in autonomous driving, are trending and drawing
extensive attention due to their capacity for comprehending driving
environments. The established world model holds immense potential for the
generation of high-quality driving videos, and driving policies for safe
maneuvering. However, a critical limitation in relevant research lies in its
predominant focus on gaming environments or simulated settings, thereby lacking
the representation of real-world driving scenarios. Therefore, we introduce
DriveDreamer, a pioneering world model entirely derived from real-world driving
scenarios. Regarding that modeling the world in intricate driving scenes
entails an overwhelming search space, we propose harnessing the powerful
diffusion model to construct a comprehensive representation of the complex
environment. Furthermore, we introduce a two-stage training pipeline. In the
initial phase, DriveDreamer acquires a deep understanding of structured traffic
constraints, while the subsequent stage equips it with the ability to
anticipate future states. The proposed DriveDreamer is the first world model
established from real-world driving scenarios. We instantiate DriveDreamer on
the challenging nuScenes benchmark, and extensive experiments verify that
DriveDreamer empowers precise, controllable video generation that faithfully
captures the structural constraints of real-world traffic scenarios.
Additionally, DriveDreamer enables the generation of realistic and reasonable
driving policies, opening avenues for interaction and practical applications.

keyInsights: Leveraging diffusion models with structured conditional inputs (HDMaps, 3D bounding boxes, text prompts) effectively manages the complexity and immense search space of real-world driving scenarios.
A two-stage training pipeline, progressively learning from static structural constraints to dynamic future prediction, enhances sampling efficiency and model convergence in real-world contexts.
The ability to model real-world driving scenarios allows for the generation of high-fidelity synthetic data, which proves valuable for improving downstream perception tasks like 3D object detection.
originalProblem: Prior world models for autonomous driving predominantly rely on gaming or simulated environments, which lack the complexity and visual fidelity of real-world scenarios.
This reliance leads to a "simulation gap" that limits the direct applicability and robustness of these models for real-world deployment.
Modeling the real world for autonomous driving involves an overwhelming search space, making it challenging to generate diverse, realistic, and controllable driving videos and actions.
results: Generates high-quality, controllable driving videos that adhere to HDMaps, 3D bounding boxes, and text prompts, demonstrating fidelity and adherence to conditions.
Improved 3D object detection performance, boosting BEVFusion's mAP by 3.0 points and NDS by 1.9 points when augmented with 4K synthetic driving images.
Achieved an average L2 trajectory error of 0.29m in open-loop planning, matching dedicated action prediction models and relatively decreasing the average collision rate by 21%.
solution: DriveDreamer introduces an Autonomous-driving Diffusion Model (Auto-DM) built upon a latent diffusion model, adapted to take spatially aligned conditions, position conditions (3D bounding boxes), and text prompts as input.

A two-stage training pipeline is employed: first, Auto-DM learns to comprehend structured traffic constraints for image/video generation; second, it anticipates future states and actions, establishing a predictive world model.

An ActionFormer module predicts future structured traffic conditions based on historical actions, and future ego-car actions are simultaneously generated by decoding visual and historical action features.
paper_summary.summary: DriveDreamer pioneers the first world model for autonomous driving derived entirely from real-world driving scenarios, leveraging diffusion models to generate high-fidelity, controllable driving videos and predict future actions. The model's generated data improves 3D object detection performance and its action prediction achieves low trajectory errors and reduced collision rates.

title:
DriveDreamer: Towards Real-world-driven World Models for Autonomous
  Driving

abstract: Recently, world models have been incorporated into the autonomous driving systems to improve the planning reliability. Existing approaches typically predict future states through appearance generation or deterministic regression, which limits their ability to capture trajectory-conditioned scene evolution and leads to unreliable action planning. To address this, we propose DynFlowDrive, a latent world model that leverages flow-based dynamics to model the transition of world states under different driving actions. By adopting the rectifiedflow formulation, the model learns a velocity field that describes how the scene state changes under different driving actions, enabling progressive prediction of future latent states. Building upon this, we further introduce a stability-aware multi-mode trajectory selection strategy that evaluates candidate trajectories according to the stability of the induced scene transitions. Extensive experiments on the nuScenes and NavSim benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements across diverse driving frameworks without introducing additional inference overhead. Source code will be abaliable at this https URL.

keyInsights: Modeling trajectory-conditioned scene evolution as a continuous dynamical system using rectified flow provides a more faithful representation of environmental dynamics than discrete one-step latent regression.
Quantifying the 'directional consistency' of latent world evolution, derived from the flow model, offers a robust criterion for selecting safer and more physically plausible trajectories.

The benefits of the dynamic world model and stability-aware selection can be implicitly learned during training to enhance planning performance without introducing additional computational overhead during real-time inference.
originalProblem: Autonomous driving systems face challenges in predicting safe and reliable future trajectories due to the highly interactive nature of driving environments, where ego-vehicle actions influence surrounding responses.
Existing latent-space world models typically rely on one-step regression, overlooking the continuous, dynamic transition process and failing to capture trajectory-dependent dynamics.

Current trajectory evaluation criteria often lack the ability to assess the smoothness and physical plausibility of the induced environmental evolution, potentially leading to suboptimal or unsafe planning decisions.
results: On the nuScenes benchmark, DynFlowDrive reduced the average L2 displacement error by 0.4 meters and the average collision rate by 26% compared to baseline latent world models.
The approach achieved a competitive PDM Score of 88.7% on the NavSim closed-loop benchmark, outperforming other world model-based methods like WoTE, LAW, and World4Drive.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the dynamic latent world model and the stability-aware multi-mode selection mechanism independently and synergistically contribute to improved trajectory accuracy and safety.
solution: DynFlowDrive introduces a flow-based latent world model that explicitly formulates trajectory-conditioned scene evolution as a continuous dynamical system.
The method integrates a pretrained foundation encoder to extract stable, dynamics-aligned latent representations of the world, which are then used for flow-based simulation.
A stability-aware multi-mode selection mechanism combines trajectory error, latent reconstruction discrepancy, and flow directional consistency to identify the most robust and plausible trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Zhejiang University and National University of Singapore developed DynFlowDrive, a flow-based latent world model that explicitly formulates trajectory-conditioned scene evolution as a continuous dynamical system for autonomous driving. This approach reduced the L2 displacement error by 0.4 meters and the collision rate by 26% on the nuScenes benchmark while improving planning reliability without inference overhead.

title: DynFlowDrive: Flow-Based Dynamic World Modeling for Autonomous Driving

abstract:
In autonomous driving, predicting future events in advance and evaluating the
foreseeable risks empowers autonomous vehicles to better plan their actions,
enhancing safety and efficiency on the road. To this end, we propose Drive-WM,
the first driving world model compatible with existing end-to-end planning
models. Through a joint spatial-temporal modeling facilitated by view
factorization, our model generates high-fidelity multiview videos in driving
scenes. Building on its powerful generation ability, we showcase the potential
of applying the world model for safe driving planning for the first time.
Particularly, our Drive-WM enables driving into multiple futures based on
distinct driving maneuvers, and determines the optimal trajectory according to
the image-based rewards. Evaluation on real-world driving datasets verifies
that our method could generate high-quality, consistent, and controllable
multiview videos, opening up possibilities for real-world simulations and safe
planning.

keyInsights: A joint factorization method ensures multiview consistency in generated videos by dividing views into reference and stitched views, then generating stitched views conditioned on neighbors.
A unified cross-attention mechanism enables the model to incorporate diverse conditions, including initial context images, text descriptions, 3D object layouts, action sequences, and HD maps.
The integration of foundation models like GPT-4V for reward functions allows for non-vectorized, context-aware assessment of imagined driving scenarios, enhancing planning evaluation.
originalProblem: Current end-to-end autonomous driving systems lack the ability to anticipate the consequences of their actions before execution, which is crucial for safety and reliability.
Traditional modular autonomous driving approaches face challenges with error propagation between components and struggle to handle complex or unusual driving situations effectively.
Prior world models were limited to simplified environments and lacked the fidelity and control required for realistic, complex real-world autonomous driving scenarios.
results: Drive-WM generates high-quality multiview videos, outperforming previous methods in Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and Fréchet Video Distance (FVD).
The model demonstrates improved multiview consistency, quantified by a novel Keypoint Matching (KPM) metric, compared to baseline methods.
When integrated into a planning system, Drive-WM enhances performance in both normal and out-of-distribution driving scenarios, showing improved robustness in unusual situations.
solution: Drive-WM is a high-fidelity, controllable, multiview world model for autonomous driving that generates realistic future scenarios based on actions.
It employs a 3D UNet architecture with frame-wise cross-attention and specialized multiview/temporal layers for synchronized multiview video generation.
The model integrates with a tree-based planning approach, using reward functions to evaluate imagined futures and select optimal actions for enhanced safety and robustness.
paper_summary.summary: Drive-WM is a world model for autonomous driving that enables vehicles to anticipate future scenarios by generating high-fidelity, controllable, multiview videos. It integrates with end-to-end planning systems to evaluate potential actions and improve robustness, particularly in out-of-distribution situations.

title:
Driving into the Future: Multiview Visual Forecasting and Planning with
  World Model for Autonomous Driving

abstract: Recently, world-action models (WAM) have emerged to bridge vision-language-action (VLA) models and world models, unifying their reasoning and instruction-following capabilities and spatio-temporal world modeling. However, existing WAM approaches often focus on modeling 2D appearance or latent representations, with limited geometric grounding-an essential element for embodied systems operating in the physical world. We present DriveDreamer-Policy, a unified driving world-action model that integrates depth generation, future video generation, and motion planning within a single modular architecture. The model employs a large language model to process language instructions, multi-view images, and actions, followed by three lightweight generators that produce depth, future video, and actions. By learning a geometry-aware world representation and using it to guide both future prediction and planning within a unified framework, the proposed model produces more coherent imagined futures and more informed driving actions, while maintaining modularity and controllable latency. Experiments on the Navsim v1 and v2 benchmarks demonstrate that DriveDreamer-Policy achieves strong performance on both closed-loop planning and world generation tasks. In particular, our model reaches 89.2 PDMS on Navsim v1 and 88.7 EPDMS on Navsim v2, outperforming existing world-model-based approaches while producing higher-quality future video and depth predictions. Ablation studies further show that explicit depth learning provides complementary benefits to video imagination and improves planning robustness.

keyInsights: Explicit geometric grounding, achieved through dedicated depth map generation, serves as an effective 3D scaffold that significantly improves the coherence and informativeness of future video predictions.
Jointly modeling depth, video, and actions within a single, modular architecture, guided by an LLM, leads to more robust and informed motion planning decisions.

The causal conditioning pathway and modular design allow for flexible operating modes, such as planning-only or imagination-enabled planning, while maintaining controllable latency for practical deployment.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) planners often lack explicit modeling of how the future world evolves under different actions, limiting interpretability and reliability in safety-critical scenarios.

Existing World-Action Models (WAMs) frequently lack explicit geometric grounding, relying on 2D appearance or latent representations, which can lead to geometrically inconsistent future scenarios for planning.
Autonomous driving is fundamentally a 4D physical process, necessitating accurate 3D geometry evolving over time for tasks like occlusion reasoning and ensuring physically consistent motion.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art planning performance on Navsim v1 (PDMS 89.2) and Navsim v2 (EPDMS 88.7), outperforming existing vision-based, VLA, and world-model-based approaches.

Demonstrated substantial improvements in future video generation quality, reducing the Fréchet Video Distance (FVD) by 32.36 compared to prior world models (PWM), indicating more temporally consistent predictions.

Improved depth accuracy with an Absolute Relative Error (AbsRel) of 8.1 and high threshold accuracies ($
	_1$ of 92.8), attributed to LLM conditioning that resolves local ambiguities and promotes geometric consistency.
solution: Developed DriveDreamer-Policy, a unified driving world-action model that integrates a Large Language Model (LLM) with lightweight generative experts for depth, video, and action prediction.

Implemented a causal ordering in the LLM's query processing (depth 
	 video 
	 action) to ensure subsequent generative experts leverage progressively richer contextual information, including explicit 3D geometry.

Utilized conditional flow matching for training the generative experts to produce continuous targets for depth maps, future video sequences, and action trajectories, enhancing generation quality and consistency.
paper_summary.summary: DriveDreamer-Policy introduces a geometry-grounded world-action model that unifies depth generation, future video generation, and motion planning for autonomous driving. This model achieved state-of-the-art planning performance on Navsim v1 (PDMS 89.2) and v2 (EPDMS 88.7), while also improving future video generation quality with a FVD reduction of 32.36 and enhancing depth accuracy (AbsRel 8.1).

title: DriveDreamer-Policy: A Geometry-Grounded World-Action Model for Unified Generation and Planning

abstract: Trajectory planning is a fundamental yet challenging component of autonomous driving. End-to-end planners frequently falter under adverse weather, unpredictable human behavior, or complex road layouts, primarily because they lack strong generalization or few-shot capabilities beyond their training data. We propose LLaViDA, a Large Language Vision Driving Assistant that leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for object motion prediction, semantic grounding, and chain-of-thought reasoning for trajectory planning in autonomous driving. A two-stage training pipeline--supervised fine-tuning followed by Trajectory Preference Optimization (TPO)--enhances scene understanding and trajectory planning by injecting regression-based supervision, produces a powerful "VLM Trajectory Planner for Autonomous Driving." On the NuScenes benchmark, LLaViDA surpasses state-of-the-art end-to-end and other recent VLM/LLM-based baselines in open-loop trajectory planning task, achieving an average L2 trajectory error of 0.31 m and a collision rate of 0.10% on the NuScenes test set. The code for this paper is available at GitHub.

keyInsights: Unifying perception, prediction, and planning within a single VLM can lead to highly accurate, interpretable, and robust autonomous driving systems.
Explicit natural-language reasoning, even when synthesized and validated by large language models, significantly improves VLM robustness and semantic understanding in complex driving scenarios.

Trajectory Preference Optimization (TPO) effectively bridges the gap between generative token-based models and the need for continuous, numerically precise outputs in tasks like trajectory planning by leveraging trajectory quality metrics.
Structured textual input describing key object states is more critical than purely visual conditioning for precise spatial localization in VLM-based planners, preventing hallucinations.
originalProblem: Traditional end-to-end autonomous driving systems often lack generalization, interpretability, and robustness in complex or novel scenarios.
Existing VLM/LLM approaches for autonomous driving struggle to generate numerically precise, structured trajectories in a single inference step and suffer from high latency.
A scarcity of autonomous driving datasets with structured natural-language rationales hinders VLMs from learning to explicitly reason through a scene and derive appropriate driving plans.
results: Achieved an average ℓ2 trajectory error of 0.31m over a 3s horizon and a 0.10% collision rate on the NuScenes benchmark (ST-P3 protocol), surpassing comparable VLM/LLM and modular baselines.
Trajectory Preference Optimization (TPO) yielded a substantial reduction in average ℓ2 displacement error from 0.39m (SFT only) to 0.31m (SFT+TPO).
Efficiency optimizations reduced inference latency from 2423 ms to 776 ms, making the system viable for real-time deployment with minimal performance degradation.
Demonstrated superior semantic scene understanding and explicit multi-agent motion prediction compared to baselines in qualitative case studies, leading to safer and more compliant trajectories.
solution: Develops a VLM-centric architecture that unifies perception, prediction, and planning, generating hierarchical chain-of-thought reasoning and numerically precise 3-second future trajectories in a single inference turn.
Curates NuScenes-TP, a trajectory planning dataset derived from NuScenes, enriched with natural-language reasoning labels synthesized and validated by GPT-4o.
Introduces a two-stage training pipeline comprising Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Trajectory Preference Optimization (TPO) to inject a continuous, regression-like signal for trajectory accuracy.
Incorporates efficiency optimizations like direct-output decoding, view reduction, and KV caching to achieve real-time inference latency suitable for deployment.
paper_summary.summary: LLaViDA introduces a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a unified decision-making engine for autonomous driving, generating both explicit chain-of-thought reasoning and numerically precise trajectories. It achieves an average ℓ2 trajectory error of 0.31m and a 0.10% collision rate on the NuScenes benchmark, outperforming existing methods while operating at a real-time inference latency of 776 ms.

title: LLaViDA: A Large Language Vision Driving Assistant for Explicit Reasoning and Enhanced Trajectory Planning

abstract:
Directly producing planning results from raw sensors has been a long-desired
solution for autonomous driving and has attracted increasing attention
recently. Most existing end-to-end autonomous driving methods factorize this
problem into perception, motion prediction, and planning. However, we argue
that the conventional progressive pipeline still cannot comprehensively model
the entire traffic evolution process, e.g., the future interaction between the
ego car and other traffic participants and the structural trajectory prior. In
this paper, we explore a new paradigm for end-to-end autonomous driving, where
the key is to predict how the ego car and the surroundings evolve given past
scenes. We propose GenAD, a generative framework that casts autonomous driving
into a generative modeling problem. We propose an instance-centric scene
tokenizer that first transforms the surrounding scenes into map-aware instance
tokens. We then employ a variational autoencoder to learn the future trajectory
distribution in a structural latent space for trajectory prior modeling. We
further adopt a temporal model to capture the agent and ego movements in the
latent space to generate more effective future trajectories. GenAD finally
simultaneously performs motion prediction and planning by sampling
distributions in the learned structural latent space conditioned on the
instance tokens and using the learned temporal model to generate futures.
Extensive experiments on the widely used nuScenes benchmark show that the
proposed GenAD achieves state-of-the-art performance on vision-centric
end-to-end autonomous driving with high efficiency. Code:
https://github.com/wzzheng/GenAD.

keyInsights: Modeling high-order interactions between the ego vehicle and other agents is crucial for accurate and safe planning, as evidenced by increased collision rates when this feature is removed.
Re-casting autonomous driving as a generative modeling problem enables unified prediction and planning, leveraging structural priors for more realistic trajectories.
Optimizing for an end-to-end objective within a generative model can lead to synergistic improvements across perception, prediction, and planning tasks.
originalProblem: Existing end-to-end autonomous driving systems use a serial design (perception, prediction, planning) that fails to comprehensively model the dynamic interactions between the ego vehicle and surrounding agents.
Many autonomous driving methods neglect to incorporate inherent structural priors of realistic vehicle trajectories, leading to less natural or suboptimal predicted paths.
Traditional approaches often treat motion prediction for agents and planning for the ego vehicle as separate, sequentially dependent tasks rather than a unified, mutually influencing process.
results: Achieved an average L2 planning displacement error of 0.91m on the nuScenes dataset, outperforming prior methods like UniAD (1.03m) and VAD-Base (1.22m).
Maintained a competitive average collision rate of 0.43% and high inference efficiency at 6.7 FPS on a single RTX 3090 GPU, comparable to more lightweight models.
Ablation studies confirmed that modeling ego-agent interactions is crucial for safety, with its removal increasing the average collision rate from 0.43% to 1.22%.
solution: GenAD introduces an instance-centric scene representation that uses map-aware instance tokens for the ego vehicle and other agents, enabling high-order interactions through self-attention.
A Variational Autoencoder (VAE) is employed for trajectory prior modeling, learning a structured latent space for realistic future trajectories of all instances.
A GRU-based generative model iteratively decodes future waypoints from the learned latent space, simultaneously producing ego vehicle plans and agent predictions within a unified framework.
paper_summary.summary: GenAD presents a generative end-to-end framework for autonomous driving that simultaneously models ego-agent interactions and learns structural trajectory priors from raw sensor inputs. This approach achieves an average L2 planning displacement error of 0.91m on the nuScenes dataset while maintaining high inference efficiency and a competitive collision rate.

title: GenAD: Generative End-to-End Autonomous Driving

abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving systems increasingly rely on vision-centric world models to understand and predict their environment. However, a common ineffectiveness in these models is the full reconstruction of future scenes, which expends significant capacity on redundantly modeling static backgrounds. To address this, we propose IR-WM, an Implicit Residual World Model that focuses on modeling the current state and evolution of the world. IR-WM first establishes a robust bird's-eye-view representation of the current state from the visual observation. It then leverages the BEV features from the previous timestep as a strong temporal prior and predicts only the "residual", i.e., the changes conditioned on the ego-vehicle's actions and scene context. To alleviate error accumulation over time, we further apply an alignment module to calibrate semantic and dynamic misalignments. Moreover, we investigate different forecasting-planning coupling schemes and demonstrate that the implicit future state generated by world models substantially improves planning accuracy. On the nuScenes benchmark, IR-WM achieves top performance in both 4D occupancy forecasting and trajectory planning.
title: Vision-Centric 4D Occupancy Forecasting and Planning via Implicit Residual World Models

abstract: Autonomous driving requires generating safe and reliable trajectories from complex multimodal inputs. Traditional modular pipelines separate perception, prediction, and planning, while recent end-to-end (E2E) systems learn them jointly. Vision-language models (VLMs) further enrich this paradigm by introducing cross-modal priors and commonsense reasoning, yet current VLM-based planners face three key challenges: (i) a mismatch between discrete text reasoning and continuous control, (ii) high latency from autoregressive chain-of-thought decoding, and (iii) inefficient or non-causal planners that limit real-time deployment. We propose ColaVLA, a unified vision-language-action framework that transfers reasoning from text to a unified latent space and couples it with a hierarchical, parallel trajectory decoder. The Cognitive Latent Reasoner compresses scene understanding into compact, decision-oriented meta-action embeddings through ego-adaptive selection and only two VLM forward passes. The Hierarchical Parallel Planner then generates multi-scale, causality-consistent trajectories in a single forward pass. Together, these components preserve the generalization and interpretability of VLMs while enabling efficient, accurate and safe trajectory generation. Experiments on the nuScenes benchmark show that ColaVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance in both open-loop and closed-loop settings with favorable efficiency and robustness.

keyInsights: VLM reasoning can be effectively performed in a continuous latent space, which maintains knowledge priors while significantly mitigating modality mismatch and reducing inference latency compared to discrete text-based methods.
A causally-preserving hierarchical planning architecture, which refines trajectories from coarse to fine scales with specific attention masks, yields smoother, more accurate, and safer motion plans.

The use of an ego-adaptive router to identify and focus on safety-critical visual tokens for latent reasoning can significantly improve efficiency and decision relevance without sacrificing comprehensive scene understanding.
originalProblem: Existing VLM-based planners suffer from a modality mismatch where discrete text reasoning struggles to align with continuous vehicular dynamics and geometric trajectories, potentially leading to physically inconsistent outputs.
High inference latency from autoregressive chain-of-thought (CoT) decoding in VLMs, where sequential token-by-token generation makes systems unsuitable for real-time autonomous driving constraints.
Many current VLM-based planning approaches are computationally inefficient or do not inherently preserve causal structure during trajectory generation, limiting their practical application.
results: ColaVLA achieved state-of-the-art performance on the nuScenes open-loop benchmark with the lowest average L2 displacement error of 0.30m and a 23% reduction in collision rate (0.23%) compared to the previous best action-based baseline.

In closed-loop NeuroNCAP simulations, the model established a new state-of-the-art score of 3.48, significantly reducing the average collision rate from 65.1% to 36.8%, demonstrating enhanced robustness in safety-critical scenarios.

The framework demonstrated an end-to-end inference latency of 727ms per frame, achieving over 5 times faster inference than text-based VLM models, which typically require more than 3700ms, making it suitable for real-time deployment.
solution: The Cognitive Latent Reasoner relocates VLM reasoning from explicit textual chains-of-thought to a unified latent space, distilling multimodal scene understanding into compact, decision-oriented meta-action embeddings.

A Hierarchical Parallel Planner generates trajectories by progressively refining motion plans from coarse to fine scales, integrating temporal abstraction, causality-preserving hybrid attention, and confidence-guided parallel decoding.

An ego-adaptive router is introduced within the reasoner to select top-K safety-critical visual tokens, compacting scene understanding and enhancing efficiency for subsequent reasoning and planning stages.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces ColaVLA, a unified Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework for autonomous driving that leverages cognitive latent reasoning for hierarchical parallel trajectory planning. The framework achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and safety in both open-loop and closed-loop nuScenes evaluations while reducing inference latency by over 5 times compared to previous text-based VLM methods.

title: ColaVLA: Leveraging Cognitive Latent Reasoning for Hierarchical Parallel Trajectory Planning in Autonomous Driving

abstract: World models have gained significant attention as a promising approach for autonomous driving. By emulating human-like perception and decision-making processes, these models can predict and adapt to dynamic environments. Existing methods typically map high-dimensional observations into compact latent spaces and learn optimal policies within these latent representations. However, prior work usually jointly learns ego-vehicle dynamics and environmental transition dynamics from the image input, leading to inefficiencies and a lack of robustness to variations in vehicle dynamics. To address these issues, we propose the Vehicle Dynamics embedded Dreamer (VDD) method, which decouples the modeling of ego-vehicle dynamics from environmental transition dynamics. This separation allows the world model to generalize effectively across vehicles with diverse parameters. Additionally, we introduce two strategies to further enhance the robustness of the learned policy: Policy Adjustment during Deployment (PAD) and Policy Augmentation during Training (PAT). Comprehensive experiments in simulated environments demonstrate that the proposed model significantly improves both driving performance and robustness to variations in vehicle dynamics, outperforming existing approaches.

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling ego-vehicle dynamics from environmental dynamics within a world model significantly enhances generalization and robustness to dynamics shifts in autonomous driving.

Proactively augmenting training with imagined diverse vehicle dynamics (PAT) allows the world model to learn policies that are inherently adaptable to a wide range of physical variations, eliminating the need for retraining.
Combining a goal-based planning approach with a feedback controller provides smoother actions and contributes to overall robustness against ego-vehicle dynamics changes.
originalProblem: Existing world models for autonomous driving struggle with domain shifts, particularly variations in ego-vehicle dynamics (e.g., mass, steering limits).

Current end-to-end learning models often implicitly learn ego-vehicle dynamics intertwined with environmental dynamics from high-dimensional sensor inputs, leading to inefficiency and brittleness when vehicle characteristics change.
Autonomous driving policies frequently lack the ability to generalize across different vehicle platforms or varying vehicle loads without extensive retraining.
results: VDD consistently outperformed rule-based and model-free RL methods, and achieved a 17% increase in success rate over DreamerV3 on challenging MetaDrive roundabout maps.

VDD-PAT demonstrated significantly superior robustness, maintaining high cumulative rewards and success rates in environments with severely restricted maximum steering angles (e.g., scaled to 0.5), where baselines largely failed.
VDD showed a much smaller increase in Bayesian surprise in shifted environments compared to DreamerV3, indicating better model consistency and robustness to dynamics changes.
solution: The Vehicle Dynamics embedded Dreamer (VDD) method extends the Dreamer framework with a hierarchical context-aware RSSM (hcRSSM) that explicitly separates ego-vehicle dynamics (modeled by a parameterized physical model) from environmental dynamics.
A goal-based behavior learning approach is employed, where an actor outputs high-level goal states that a PID controller translates into actions, promoting smoother driving and robustness.

Policy Adjustment during Deployment (PAD) adapts actions at runtime, while Policy Augmentation during Training (PAT) proactively trains the world model with diverse imagined vehicle dynamics to create inherently robust policies.
paper_summary.summary: The Vehicle Dynamics embedded Dreamer (VDD) method introduces a world model that explicitly decouples ego-vehicle and environmental dynamics to improve the robustness and generalization of autonomous driving policies. It achieved superior performance in environments with varied vehicle dynamics, demonstrating higher success rates and cumulative rewards compared to prior models.

title: Vehicle Dynamics Embedded World Models for Autonomous Driving

abstract: World models envision potential future states based on various ego actions. They embed extensive knowledge about the driving environment, facilitating safe and scalable autonomous driving. Most existing methods primarily focus on either data generation or the pretraining paradigms of world models. Unlike the aforementioned prior works, we propose Drive-OccWorld, which adapts a vision-centric 4D forecasting world model to end-to-end planning for autonomous driving. Specifically, we first introduce a semantic and motion-conditional normalization in the memory module, which accumulates semantic and dynamic information from historical BEV embeddings. These BEV features are then conveyed to the world decoder for future occupancy and flow forecasting, considering both geometry and spatiotemporal modeling. Additionally, we propose injecting flexible action conditions, such as velocity, steering angle, trajectory, and commands, into the world model to enable controllable generation and facilitate a broader range of downstream applications. Furthermore, we explore integrating the generative capabilities of the 4D world model with end-to-end planning, enabling continuous forecasting of future states and the selection of optimal trajectories using an occupancy-based cost function. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the nuScenes, nuScenes-Occupancy, and Lyft-Level5 datasets illustrate that our method can generate plausible and controllable 4D occupancy, paving the way for advancements in driving world generation and end-to-end planning. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Directly integrating action-controllable 4D occupancy forecasting world models into the end-to-end planning loop significantly enhances safety, robustness, and generalization for autonomous driving.
